answer:
Yes — there is a substantial research literature on this, and the broad result is fairly consistent:

**Once pay is already “good enough,” differences in the intrinsic quality of the work** — how interesting, meaningful, autonomous, challenging, and fitting it is — **usually predict sustained job satisfaction and well-being more strongly than modest differences in pay.**

That is not a moral claim; it’s the center of gravity of several research literatures.

## The short version

Across organizational psychology, behavioral economics, and motivation research:

- **Interesting / meaningful / autonomy-rich work** is strongly associated with  
  - higher **job satisfaction**
  - higher **engagement**
  - lower **turnover intentions**
  - better **well-being**
- **Pay** does matter, but its relationship with **job satisfaction** is usually **small to modest**, especially once basic financial needs and perceived fairness are covered.
- **Promotions / status / leadership** can increase satisfaction for some people, but the average gain is often **smaller and less durable** than people expect, and sometimes comes with more stress and longer hours.
- Direct research on **“regret” after choosing a more interesting vs better-paid job** is much thinner than research on satisfaction and well-being. But research on **affective forecasting** suggests people often **overestimate** the long-term happiness gains from money/status and **underestimate** the importance of day-to-day experience.

So if your question is:

> “Do people who stay in more interesting jobs tend to be happier?”

**On average, yes — especially in terms of day-to-day job satisfaction and engagement.**  
Not always, and not if the “interesting” job is also exhausting or badly structured. But the overall evidence leans that way.

---

# What the research says

## 1) Pay matters less for job satisfaction than many people assume

One of the most directly relevant findings:

### **Judge et al. (2010), meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction**
This is a well-known meta-analysis in vocational / organizational psychology.

**Main result:**  
The relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but modest**.

Interpretation:
- Higher pay helps.
- But it does **not** buy nearly as much job satisfaction as people often expect.
- Especially among already well-paid professionals, extra pay tends to have **diminishing returns**.

This is one of the most robust findings relevant to your situation: a **bit higher salary** usually should not be expected to dramatically improve how happy you are with your work.

Related economics research also shows:
- people adapt to higher income,
- relative comparisons matter a lot,
- and absolute income has weaker effects on experienced well-being than on “life evaluation.”

### Related work
- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)**: higher income improves life evaluation more clearly than everyday emotional well-being.
- Later work, e.g. **Killingsworth (2021)**, suggests the plateau is less sharp than originally argued — but still, few serious researchers think modest salary differences dominate daily work experience once income is already high.

**Bottom line:** pay matters, but its effect on *experienced* work happiness is often overstated.

---

## 2) “Interesting work” is usually studied as autonomy, variety, challenge, significance, and fit

Research doesn’t usually use the exact term “interesting” as a single variable. It breaks it down into things like:

- **autonomy**
- **skill variety**
- **task significance**
- **task identity**
- **feedback**
- **learning/challenge**
- **meaningfulness**
- **person-job fit**

### **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007), meta-analysis on work design**
This is a major meta-analysis.

**Main result:**  
Core job characteristics like **autonomy**, **variety**, **significance**, and **feedback** are strongly related to:
- job satisfaction
- internal motivation
- performance
- lower stress / strain in some cases

These are precisely the features that often make work feel “interesting.”

In plain English:
- People tend to be happier when the work itself is engaging,
- when they have control,
- when they can use their abilities,
- and when the work feels consequential.

This literature is one of the strongest reasons researchers would take “interesting work” very seriously in a career choice.

---

## 3) Meaningful work has a robust relationship with well-being

### **Allan et al. (meta-analysis on meaningful work)**
The meaningful-work literature consistently finds that people who experience their work as meaningful report:
- higher job satisfaction
- higher life satisfaction
- more engagement
- stronger commitment
- less desire to quit

This does **not** mean “follow your passion no matter what.”  
But it does mean that **meaningful / absorbing work is not a soft variable** — it has a measurable, robust association with well-being.

For highly skilled knowledge workers, this often matters a lot because day-to-day cognitive experience is a big part of life quality.

---

## 4) Person-job fit is one of the strongest predictors of satisfaction

### **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005), meta-analysis on person-environment fit**
This meta-analysis looked at how well a person matches:
- the job
- the organization
- the team
- the supervisor

**Main result:**  
Better fit is associated with substantially higher:
- job satisfaction
- commitment
- lower intent to leave

This matters because what feels “interesting” is often really a signal of **fit**:
- fit with your strengths,
- fit with your curiosity,
- fit with your preferred way of working.

So if your current role feels much more interesting, that is likely not trivial — it may indicate better fit, and fit is a strong predictor of satisfaction.

---

## 5) Intrinsic motives tend to predict better well-being than extrinsic motives

A related literature compares **intrinsic** vs **extrinsic** values.

### Self-determination theory / work values research
Researchers such as **Deci**, **Ryan**, **Gagné**, **Vansteenkiste**, and others have repeatedly found:

When people are more oriented toward:
- growth
- learning
- interest
- mastery
- contribution
- autonomy

they tend to report better:
- vitality
- satisfaction
- engagement
- psychological well-being

When they are more oriented primarily toward:
- money
- status
- image
- external rewards

those goals are often less effective at producing durable well-being.

That does **not** mean money or status are bad.  
It means that **choices dominated by extrinsic motives** tend to produce less sustained satisfaction than choices aligned with intrinsic motives.

This is one of the cleanest answers to your question.

---

## 6) Leadership/promotion is more complicated than people assume

This part is especially relevant to your case, because your offer is not just “more money for less interesting work”; it also includes **leading a small team**, which you say you’ve always wanted.

Research on promotion and managerial responsibility is **more mixed** than research on intrinsic work content.

Broadly:
- Leadership can increase satisfaction if it brings **autonomy, influence, growth, and identity fit**.
- But it can also increase **stress, hours, role conflict, and administrative burden**.
- The net effect depends a lot on whether the person genuinely wants and fits the leadership role.

So the literature would not say:
> “Interesting IC work always beats management.”

Instead, it would say something more like:
> A leadership role can improve satisfaction if leadership itself is intrinsically meaningful to you — not just as status, but as a desired mode of work.

That distinction matters.

In other words, in research terms, your choice is probably **not** simply:
- intrinsic vs extrinsic

It may actually be:
- **one type of intrinsic value** (technical/research interest)
vs
- **another intrinsic value** (leadership/growth/influence)
plus some extrinsic upside (pay)

That makes the evidence less one-sided than if the offer were just “more money, same type of work, less interesting.”

---

## 7) People often mispredict what will make them happier

This speaks most directly to your question about regret.

There is a large literature on **affective forecasting** showing that people are systematically imperfect at predicting future happiness.

Relevant recurring findings:
- people **overweight salient differences** like pay, prestige, and title,
- people **underweight daily experience**,
- people adapt to many external gains faster than they expect.

That doesn’t give a direct “regret rate” for job choice, but it suggests a common failure mode:
- choosing based on the most legible external differences,
- then discovering that what dominates happiness is what the work actually feels like every day.

This is one reason researchers are often skeptical of large predicted well-being gains from a pay/title bump when the day-to-day work becomes less engaging.

---

# So: do people in more interesting jobs tend to be happier?

**Average answer from the literature:** yes, usually.

More precisely:

### They tend to report:
- higher **job satisfaction**
- higher **engagement**
- higher **meaning**
- lower **turnover intentions**

### But with caveats:
Interesting work does **not** guarantee happiness if it also has:
- unsustainable workload
- low autonomy
- toxic management
- chronic uncertainty
- poor team fit
- high pressure and burnout

There is also evidence from the **challenge stressor** literature that:
- challenging work can be motivating,
- but high demands can also increase strain.

So “interesting” helps a lot, but **interesting + exhausting + low control** is not a recipe for well-being.

---

# What about regret?

This is the weakest part of the evidence base.

I do **not** know of a strong, canonical research line directly answering:

> “Do people regret staying in the more interesting job rather than taking the somewhat higher-paid, less interesting one?”

What the literature gives instead is indirect evidence:

1. **Intrinsic work quality predicts satisfaction better than small pay differences.**
2. **People adapt to extrinsic gains.**
3. **People often mispredict the long-term impact of money/status.**
4. **Fit and meaning are robust predictors of work well-being.**

Taken together, that suggests:
- people are at real risk of **overvaluing salary/title**
- and **undervaluing the quality of the work itself**

But that is an inference from multiple literatures, not a clean direct “regret study.”

---

# If I were to compress the research into one sentence

For already well-compensated professionals, **the day-to-day character of the work** — especially **interest, fit, autonomy, and meaning** — is usually a stronger predictor of sustained job happiness than a modest salary increase; leadership opportunities matter too, but mostly when they are themselves intrinsically meaningful and not just prestigious.

---

# The most relevant papers / literatures to look at

If you want a compact reading list, I’d start with these:

1. **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**  
   *The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis*  
   → pay matters, but only modestly for job satisfaction

2. **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**  
   *Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary...*  
   → autonomy, variety, significance, feedback strongly predict satisfaction and motivation

3. **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**  
   *Consequences of individuals’ fit at work: A meta-analysis of person-job, person-organization, person-group, and person-supervisor fit*  
   → fit strongly predicts satisfaction and lower quitting intentions

4. **Allan et al.**  
   *Outcomes of meaningful work: A meta-analysis*  
   → meaningful work is associated with higher well-being and engagement

5. **Cerasoli, Nicklin, & Ford (2014)**  
   *Intrinsic motivation and extrinsic incentives jointly predict performance: A 40-year meta-analysis*  
   → intrinsic motivation is very important, especially for quality/creativity

6. **Gagné & Deci (2005)** and broader **self-determination theory** work  
   → autonomy, competence, relatedness predict motivation and well-being

7. **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)**, plus later updates like **Killingsworth (2021)**  
   → money matters, but not in a simple “more = happier every day” way

---

# One nuance especially relevant to your case

Because you say **leading a small team is something you always wanted**, the literature would likely treat that as a serious positive — not just as “status.”

So the research does **not** cleanly reduce your situation to:

- interesting work vs more money

It may actually be:

- **technical/research interest**
vs
- **leadership aspiration + somewhat more money**
with less interesting subject matter

That’s a more balanced trade-off, because **leadership aspiration can also be an intrinsic motivator**.

---

If you want, I can also do one of two follow-ups:

1. give you a **more academic summary with effect sizes and exact citations**, or  
2. translate the literature into a **decision framework** that stays strictly evidence-based.

answer:
Yes—there is quite a lot of research relevant to this, though not usually framed as “interesting job vs slightly higher-paying team lead role at a top lab.” The closest literatures are:

- **job design / work characteristics**
- **meaningful work / intrinsic motivation**
- **pay and well-being**
- **person–job fit**
- **promotions / managerial transitions**
- **regret and intrinsic vs extrinsic goals**

## Short version

Across organizational psychology and economics, the most consistent finding is:

> **Interesting, meaningful, autonomous work tends to predict job satisfaction and day-to-day well-being more strongly than modest differences in pay.**

A few caveats:

- **Money still matters**, especially when pay is low or perceived as unfair.
- At **already high income levels**, extra salary usually has **smaller marginal effects** on happiness than differences in the nature of the work.
- **Leadership opportunities can matter a lot** if leadership itself is part of what makes work meaningful to you. In that case, “leading a team” is not just an external reward—it is part of the intrinsic fit of the role.
- Direct evidence on **later regret** from this exact kind of choice is thinner than the evidence on satisfaction and well-being.

---

## 1) Interesting work: strong evidence it matters a lot

In the classic **Job Characteristics Model** and later meta-analyses, jobs are more satisfying and motivating when they have things like:

- **skill variety**
- **task significance**
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**
- **task identity**

These are pretty close to what many people mean by “interesting work.”

### Key findings
Meta-analytic work has repeatedly found that these job characteristics are **moderately to strongly related** to:

- **job satisfaction**
- **internal motivation**
- **engagement**
- **lower turnover intentions**
- often **better performance**

Two well-known sources:

- **Fried & Ferris (1987)** — meta-analysis supporting major parts of the Job Characteristics Model.
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — larger meta-analysis showing work design features such as autonomy, variety, significance, and social characteristics are strongly tied to satisfaction and motivation.

A practical summary of that literature is:

> People tend to be happier in jobs that are intrinsically engaging than in jobs that are merely better rewarded.

And importantly, the effect sizes for work characteristics are often **larger than the pay–satisfaction link**.

---

## 2) Meaningful / intrinsically motivating work predicts well-being

Another relevant literature is on **meaningful work** and **intrinsic motivation**.

### Meaningful work
Reviews and meta-analyses generally find that meaningful work is associated with:

- higher **job satisfaction**
- higher **work engagement**
- greater **organizational commitment**
- better **psychological well-being**
- lower **burnout** and **turnover intentions**

A useful reference here is:

- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)** — meta-analysis on outcomes of meaningful work.

### Self-determination theory
Self-determination research also shows that people tend to do better when their activities satisfy needs for:

- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

That is one reason interesting work often matters so much: it supports autonomy and competence directly.

A broader related result from motivation research is that people who orient more toward **intrinsic goals** (growth, contribution, relationships, mastery) generally report better well-being than people primarily focused on **extrinsic goals** (money, status, image).

Classic sources:
- **Kasser & Ryan** on intrinsic vs extrinsic aspirations
- broader **Self-Determination Theory** literature (Deci & Ryan)

This does **not** mean money or status do not matter; it means they are usually weaker long-run predictors of well-being than work that feels engaging and self-concordant.

---

## 3) Salary matters, but usually less than people expect

There is strong evidence that **pay is positively related to job satisfaction**, but the relationship is **smaller than many people assume**.

### Key finding
- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)** found in a meta-analysis that the relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but modest**.

The usual interpretation is:

- Pay helps
- But it is far from the main driver of whether people like their jobs

This becomes especially relevant in your case because you are almost certainly already in a **very high pay band**. The literature on income and subjective well-being suggests that:

- increases in income generally do increase well-being,
- but **marginal gains get smaller**, especially once basic needs and comfort are long solved,
- and **relative comparisons** / fairness matter a lot.

Relevant papers:
- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** — famous finding that life evaluation rises with income, while emotional well-being showed a plateau in their data
- **Killingsworth (2021)** — later evidence suggesting experienced well-being continues to rise with income for many people
- later work reconciles these somewhat, but the broad takeaway is still:  
  **income matters, but not in a way that usually dominates work content once income is already high**

For someone choosing between **“very high pay” and “a bit higher pay”**, the literature would generally predict that the salary difference alone is **unlikely** to be the main determinant of happiness.

---

## 4) Person–job fit may be the most important caveat in your case

This is where your case gets more nuanced.

You said that **leading a small team is something you always wanted to do**.

That matters a lot, because then the tradeoff is not just:

- interesting work vs more money

It is also:

- interesting technical work vs a role that may fit a **deep career aspiration**

The research on **person–job fit** and **person–organization fit** is relevant here.

### Key finding
Meta-analytic work shows that better fit predicts:

- higher **job satisfaction**
- stronger **commitment**
- lower **turnover intentions**
- better well-being and performance

Important source:
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** — meta-analysis on fit at work

So the research-based way to think about your situation is:

> If “becoming a leader” is central to your identity, then the OpenAI role may not actually be lower on intrinsic value overall, even if the technical content is less interesting.

But this depends on whether you want the **reality** of management, not just the idea of leadership.

Research on management transitions often finds that promotions can bring:

- more status
- more pay
- more influence

but also:

- more stress
- less autonomy over deep work
- more meetings, conflict resolution, hiring, performance management, coordination, politics

That means people can want “leadership” abstractly but dislike the actual day-to-day of management.

This is one reason some firms created **dual career ladders**: the evidence is pretty clear that top technical contributors and effective managers are not interchangeable.

---

## 5) Promotions often do not create lasting happiness by themselves

There is a relevant line of longitudinal research suggesting that **promotions** can improve satisfaction in the short run, but the effects are often not as large or lasting as people expect, and may be offset by greater stress/workload.

So if part of the appeal of the OpenAI role is:
- title
- status
- “finally managing”
- external prestige

the literature would say: be careful not to overestimate the durable happiness from those alone.

The durable part usually comes from:
- whether the actual work fits you,
- whether you find the role meaningful,
- and whether the day-to-day is energizing rather than draining.

---

## 6) Do people regret staying in the more interesting job?

Here the evidence is less direct.

I’m not aware of a strong body of research that specifically tracks people who choose **more interesting work over a better-paid managerial role** and then asks who later regrets it more.

But the broader regret literature suggests two things:

### 1. Long-run regrets are often about not pursuing meaningful or identity-relevant possibilities
Classic regret research finds that over time, people often regret **inactions** more than actions, especially when those inactions relate to important possible selves.

- **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)** — actions hurt more in the short term, inactions are often the larger long-run regrets.
- **Roese & Summerville (2005)** — major life regrets cluster around domains like education, career, romance, self-development.

### 2. Regret tends to be strongest when the foregone path feels central to who you wanted to become
So if “I always wanted to lead” is genuinely central, not casual, then passing it up could plausibly be the sort of thing people later regret.

But equally:

- if you leave highly engaging work for a role whose daily content you find dull,
- and the leadership component turns out less fulfilling than imagined,

that can also become a source of regret.

So the regret literature does **not** cleanly say “choose interesting work” or “choose the stretch role.” It says:

> Long-run regret is often more about failing to pursue a deeply valued identity-relevant goal than about missing a modest financial improvement.

---

## 7) What the research would predict in a situation like yours

Very roughly, if I translate the literature into predictions:

### If the differences are:
- **current job**: much more interesting
- **new job**: a bit more money, first management role, but substantially less interesting

then research would generally predict:

### You are more likely to be happier in the long run if:
- the work itself stays intellectually engaging,
- you have autonomy,
- you feel growth/meaning,
- and the salary difference is not life-changing.

### You may be happier taking the new role if:
- leading people is itself deeply meaningful to you,
- you want to shift from individual research contribution to enabling others,
- you’re excited by coaching, hiring, alignment, prioritization, and ambiguity,
- and you would feel a lasting sense of missed identity development if you never try management.

In other words, the research suggests that the real comparison is not:

- **interesting work** vs **salary**

It is closer to:

- **one kind of intrinsic reward** (intellectual interest)
vs
- **another kind of intrinsic reward** (leadership / growth / influence)

with salary being a secondary variable unless it is very large.

---

## 8) If you want the most research-grounded takeaway

If I had to compress the literature into a few claims:

1. **Interesting, meaningful, autonomous work is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction.**
2. **Pay predicts job satisfaction, but only modestly compared with work design and fit.**
3. **At high income levels, small salary differences usually matter less than people think.**
4. **Leadership can be very fulfilling if it matches your identity—but management is a different job, not just a promotion.**
5. **Long-term regret is more often tied to not pursuing important intrinsic goals than to not maximizing money.**

So yes:

> On average, people who stay in work they find genuinely interesting/meaningful do tend to be happier than people who move mainly for somewhat better pay or status.

But your case has an important exception:

> If leading a team is itself one of the things that would make work meaningful for you, the new role may still be the better fit despite being less technically interesting.

---

## A few papers/research streams worth reading

If you want to go to the sources, I’d start with these:

- **Hackman & Oldham (1976)** — Job Characteristics Model
- **Fried & Ferris (1987)** — meta-analysis of the Job Characteristics Model
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — meta-analysis on work design
- **Judge et al. (2010)** — meta-analysis of pay and job satisfaction
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** — meta-analysis on person–job/person–organization fit
- **Allan et al. (2019)** — meta-analysis on meaningful work outcomes
- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** and **Killingsworth (2021)** — income and well-being
- **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)**; **Roese & Summerville (2005)** — regret

If you want, I can also do one of two things next:

1. give you a **more academic summary with effect sizes and citations**, or  
2. turn this into a **decision framework based explicitly on the research** rather than personal advice.

answer:
Yes. The strongest result from organizational psychology is roughly:

**Once pay is already “good enough,” day-to-day job quality — especially interest, autonomy, meaning, and fit — predicts job satisfaction much more strongly than marginal increases in pay.**

A few important nuances:

1. Researchers usually do **not** study “interestingness” as a single variable. They study nearby constructs:
   - **intrinsic motivation**
   - **task variety / skill variety**
   - **autonomy**
   - **meaningfulness / task significance**
   - **person–job fit**
   - **growth / mastery opportunities**

2. The evidence on **happiness/job satisfaction** is much stronger than the evidence on **future regret**. Regret is studied, but less directly for this exact career tradeoff.

## What the research says

### 1) Intrinsic job characteristics matter a lot for satisfaction
A large literature going back to **Hackman & Oldham’s Job Characteristics Model** finds that jobs high in:
- skill variety
- task identity
- task significance
- autonomy
- feedback

produce more **internal motivation** and **job satisfaction**.

A major meta-analysis:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, meta-analysis of work design research  
  Found that motivational job characteristics were **strongly associated** with job satisfaction, internal motivation, and lower turnover intentions.

In plain English: **work that feels engaging and meaningful tends to make people like their jobs more.**

---

### 2) Meaningful work is one of the strongest predictors of positive work outcomes
There are meta-analyses showing that **meaningful work** is associated with:
- higher job satisfaction
- higher work engagement
- stronger commitment
- better general well-being
- lower burnout / lower desire to quit

For example:

- **Allan et al.** meta-analytic work on meaningful work and outcomes  
  The pattern is generally **moderate to large positive associations** with satisfaction and engagement, and negative associations with withdrawal/burnout-type outcomes.

So if by “interesting” you mean “I genuinely care about the problems and want to think about them,” that is not a trivial preference. In the literature, it is a major determinant of work well-being.

---

### 3) Pay matters, but much less than people often expect
The classic meta-analysis here is:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**, *The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis*  
  Main result: **the correlation between pay level and job satisfaction is positive but modest/small**.

That means:
- money is **not irrelevant**
- but it is **far from the dominant driver** of whether people like their jobs

This becomes especially relevant when someone is already highly compensated. A move from “good pay” to “slightly better pay” generally does **less** for satisfaction than a move from “boring/misaligned” to “interesting/aligned.”

---

### 4) At high income levels, marginal income has smaller practical effects on well-being
The broader happiness literature is also relevant:

- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** found that emotional well-being rose with income mainly up to a point, while life evaluation continued beyond that.
- **Killingsworth (2021)** argued that experienced well-being may continue to rise with income on average, but this does **not** mean every additional dollar matters equally; the marginal effect is typically smaller once basic and many comfort needs are long covered.

For someone in a Google/OpenAI-level compensation band, the research would generally predict that:
- **small pay differences** matter less for happiness
- **daily work experience** matters more

---

### 5) For creative/knowledge work, intrinsic motivation is especially important
This is particularly relevant for a research engineer.

- **Cerasoli, Nicklin, & Ford (2014)**, meta-analysis on intrinsic motivation and incentives  
  A key finding: **intrinsic motivation is especially important for performance quality**, while incentives are more tied to quantity.

For research, invention, and deep technical problem-solving, liking the work often matters not only for happiness but for **performance quality and persistence**.

That does not mean salary is unimportant. It means that in cognitively demanding, creative work, **interest is unusually valuable**.

---

### 6) Person–job fit is a major factor — and leadership aspiration counts as fit
There is also strong evidence that people do better when the role fits who they are and what they want.

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**, meta-analysis on person-environment fit  
  Found substantial associations between fit and job satisfaction, commitment, and turnover intentions.

This matters for your situation because “leading a small team” is not just an extrinsic perk. If leadership is something you’ve genuinely wanted, that counts as:
- identity fit
- growth
- meaning
- self-concordance

So your tradeoff is **not simply**:
- interesting work vs money

It is closer to:
- **technical/research interest**
vs
- **leadership aspiration + some extra money**

The literature would treat both sides as psychologically meaningful, though the salary part alone is usually the weakest component.

---

### 7) Workload and burnout can outweigh both pay and interest
One more big finding from the **job demands–resources** literature:

High job demands — e.g.
- long hours
- constant urgency
- role conflict
- poor boundaries
- pressure without recovery

are strongly associated with **burnout and exhaustion**.

So if one option is less interesting **and** more intense, the evidence would tilt even more strongly against it. Conversely, a team-lead role with good autonomy and manageable demands can still be satisfying even if the topic is somewhat less intrinsically interesting.

This is worth taking seriously because in practice, workload often has a larger effect on well-being than people expect.

---

## Do people who choose the more interesting job tend to be happier?
**On average, yes — if “interesting” means high intrinsic motivation, meaning, autonomy, and fit.**

That conclusion is fairly well supported.

More precisely:
- people in jobs with richer intrinsic characteristics tend to report higher job satisfaction
- they are more engaged
- they are less likely to want to leave
- they often have better well-being

And the size of these associations is generally **larger than the association between pay and job satisfaction**.

---

## Do they regret it less?
Here the evidence is weaker.

There is **not** a clean body of research saying:
> “People who chose the more interesting job later regret it less than people who chose the higher-paying job.”

That exact question has not been studied nearly as directly.

What we do have:

- **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)** and later regret research suggest that, over the long run, people often regret **missed opportunities** and failures to pursue the path that fit their “ideal self.”
- **Roese & Summerville (2005)** found that regret is especially common in domains tied to self-definition, including career.

But I would be careful here:
- this is more general regret research
- it is not strong enough to say “interesting job choices definitively reduce regret”

So the evidence is:
- **strong** for day-to-day satisfaction and engagement
- **much weaker/more indirect** for long-run regret

---

## The most research-grounded summary for your situation

If I translate the literature into your specific tradeoff:

### What is likely true
- A **slightly higher salary** is unlikely to add much to happiness if you are already very well paid.
- **Interesting/meaningful work** is likely to matter a lot for day-to-day satisfaction.
- For a research engineer, intrinsic interest is especially important because it supports deep work and high-quality output.
- If **leading a team** is a real aspiration, that factor should be treated as a major positive, not as a superficial status marker.
- If the new role is substantially **less interesting and more intense**, that is a meaningful warning sign from the well-being literature.

### What the literature does **not** say clearly
- It does not give a universal rule like “always choose the more interesting job.”
- It does not show strong causal evidence about who regrets which path later.
- It does not tell you whether your leadership aspiration will outweigh the loss of topic interest; that depends on your personal weighting of two things the research both says matter: **intrinsic interest** and **fit with your desired future self**.

---

## If you want the most evidence-based decision lens
The literature suggests focusing less on salary difference and more on these 5 variables:

1. **Intrinsic interest in the day-to-day work**
2. **Autonomy and control**
3. **Leadership/identity fit**
4. **Workload / burnout risk**
5. **Meaning and future growth**

That is where the research says most of the happiness/satisfaction variance is likely to come from in a case like yours.

If you want, I can turn this into a **research-based decision matrix** with weights drawn from the literature, so you can compare Google vs OpenAI in a more structured way.

answer:
Yes. The short version from the research is:

- **Interesting / intrinsically motivating / meaningful work is usually a stronger predictor of sustained job satisfaction than pay level alone.**
- **Pay matters**, but its relationship to happiness and job satisfaction is typically **smaller** than the relationship for job design factors like autonomy, skill variety, feedback, and meaning.
- **Promotions / leadership roles** often produce a **smaller and less durable** happiness boost than people expect, unless they also improve autonomy, fit, and sense of growth.
- There is **less direct research on “regret”** specifically, but the longitudinal evidence generally favors **intrinsic job qualities** over **extrinsic rewards** for long-run well-being.

A more research-based breakdown:

## 1) “Interesting work” is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction

In the literature, “interesting work” is usually captured indirectly through things like:

- **intrinsic motivation**
- **meaningful work**
- **skill variety / challenge**
- **autonomy**
- **task significance**
- **opportunities to learn/master**

### Classic work-design research
**Hackman & Oldham’s Job Characteristics Model** is one of the foundational theories here. It argues that jobs are more motivating and satisfying when they provide:

- skill variety
- task identity
- task significance
- autonomy
- feedback

These create a sense of meaningfulness and responsibility, which predicts motivation and satisfaction.

### Meta-analytic evidence
A major meta-analysis:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, *Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features*  
  Across many studies, job characteristics such as **autonomy, feedback, task significance, and skill variety** had **moderate to strong associations** with job satisfaction and internal motivation.  
  Roughly speaking, these effects are often in the **.3 to .5 correlation range**, which is substantial in this field.

That is a much stronger signal than “more money = much happier,” especially among already well-paid workers.

---

## 2) Meaningful work is strongly related to well-being

There is a more recent literature specifically on **meaningful work**.

- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)**, *Outcomes of meaningful work: A meta-analysis*  
  Meaningful work was strongly associated with:
  - **job satisfaction**
  - **work engagement**
  - **life satisfaction**
  - lower **turnover intentions**

The relationship with job satisfaction was quite large by organizational-psychology standards.

So if by “interesting” you mean “this work feels engaging, important, and intellectually alive,” the evidence is pretty favorable.

---

## 3) Pay matters, but less than many people think

There are two separate literatures here:

### A. Pay and job satisfaction
- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**, *The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis*  
  They found that **pay level is positively related to job satisfaction**, but the relationship is **fairly small**.  
  The famous headline from that literature is basically: **money matters, but not nearly as much as people assume**.

A good summary of the effect is that pay level typically has a **weak-to-modest** relationship with job satisfaction, much smaller than intrinsic work characteristics.

### B. Income and overall happiness / well-being
For general happiness, the evidence is nuanced:

- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)**: emotional well-being rises with income up to a point, while life evaluation keeps rising.
- **Killingsworth (2021/2023)**: happiness keeps rising with income more continuously than Kahneman & Deaton suggested, but with **diminishing returns**.

The important part for your situation is this:
If you are already at a **very high income level**, a **modest additional salary increase** is unlikely, on average, to buy as much well-being as a major difference in daily work quality.

---

## 4) Intrinsic motives tend to predict better long-run well-being than extrinsic ones

A separate but relevant literature compares **intrinsic goals** (growth, mastery, relationships, contribution) with **extrinsic goals** (money, image, status).

- **Kasser & Ryan** and later work in **Self-Determination Theory** consistently finds that people who prioritize intrinsic aspirations tend to report **better well-being** than those who put more weight on extrinsic rewards alone.

At work, SDT says that well-being depends heavily on whether the job supports:
- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

A role can be high-status and higher-paid, but if it reduces autonomy, competence-expression, or felt meaning, that often hurts well-being.

This is relevant because “leading a small team” may not be purely extrinsic. If leadership fulfills **growth, agency, impact, and relatedness**, it can count as an **intrinsic or internalized** value, not just a status reward.

So the research does **not** say “interesting work always beats leadership.”  
It says: **intrinsic fit beats extrinsic reward**.  
Leadership can be intrinsically rewarding if it is part of your real motivational structure.

---

## 5) Promotions and management roles are not a guaranteed happiness upgrade

There is some panel-data research showing that promotions improve job satisfaction, but usually:

- the effect is **modest**
- often strongest for **pay satisfaction** and status
- the boost can be **temporary**
- promotions can also increase **hours, stress, conflict, and administrative burden**

One useful paper:

- **Kosteas (2011)**, *Job satisfaction and promotions*  
  Promotions do tend to raise satisfaction, but not dramatically, and the effect interacts with whether the promotion also improves compensation and job conditions.

This matters because many people imagine:
> “If I finally lead a team, I’ll feel much better.”

The literature would say:
> Maybe — but mostly if the role also improves autonomy, fit, growth, and meaning, not just title and pay.

That is especially important in technical fields, where moving into management can mean **less contact with the intellectually engaging part of the work**.

---

## 6) What about regret?

This is the weakest part of the literature. There is much more research on:

- job satisfaction
- turnover intentions
- burnout
- meaning
- intrinsic vs extrinsic motivation

than on explicit long-term **career regret** after choosing one offer over another.

So I would not claim there is a strong direct answer like:
> “People who choose more interesting work later regret it less.”

That specific question has less direct evidence.

What we *do* know is:

- people in more meaningful/autonomous jobs report **higher satisfaction**
- they show **lower turnover intentions**
- person-job fit and interest fit predict **better long-run outcomes**
- extrinsic gains alone often produce **adaptation**: people get used to the pay/title

So the evidence does **not** suggest a common “you’ll regret choosing interesting work” pattern.  
If anything, the literature tilts in the other direction: **daily work quality tends to matter a lot**, and people adapt faster to money/status than they expect.

---

## 7) If I translated the literature into your specific situation

Not as personal advice, just as an evidence-based inference:

If you are already in a highly paid role at Google, then on average:

- the **extra salary** from OpenAI is likely to have a **modest** effect on well-being
- the difference in **how interesting the work feels every day** could have a **larger** effect
- the **leadership opportunity** could matter a lot **if** leading people is a deeply valued part of your identity, not just a prestige marker

So the research-based question is less:

> “Interesting work or higher pay?”

and more:

> “Which option gives me more of the intrinsic drivers that predict sustained well-being: autonomy, competence, growth, meaning, and relationships?”

If leading a team is something you “always wanted,” that may itself be an intrinsic-growth factor.  
If the new role is “much less interesting,” that is a serious negative in the literature, not a minor one.

---

## 8) A compact summary

### What predicts happiness at work more strongly?
**Usually stronger:**
- interesting/challenging work
- autonomy
- meaning
- learning/growth
- good relationships
- person-job fit

**Usually weaker:**
- pay level alone
- title/status alone

**Mixed / context-dependent:**
- promotion to management
- bigger scope
- prestige of employer

---

## 9) Key references you could actually look up

- **Hackman, J. R., & Oldham, G. R.** (1976/1980). Job Characteristics Model.
- **Humphrey, S. E., Nahrgang, J. D., & Morgeson, F. P.** (2007). *Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary and theoretical extension of the work design literature.*
- **Judge, T. A., Piccolo, R. F., Podsakoff, N. P., Shaw, J. C., & Rich, B. L.** (2010). *The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis of the literature.*
- **Allan, B. A., Batz-Barbarich, C., Sterling, H. M., & Tay, L.** (2019). *Outcomes of meaningful work: A meta-analysis.*
- **Kosteas, V. D.** (2011). *Job satisfaction and promotions.*
- **Kahneman, D., & Deaton, A.** (2010). *High income improves evaluation of life but not emotional well-being.*
- **Killingsworth, M. A.** (2021 / later follow-ups). Income and experienced well-being.
- **Kasser, T., & Ryan, R. M.** on intrinsic vs extrinsic aspirations.
- **Deci, E. L., & Ryan, R. M.** Self-Determination Theory; plus later workplace reviews by **Van den Broeck** and others.

If you want, I can do one of two things next:

1. give you a **curated reading list** with 8–12 papers and the main finding from each, or  
2. turn this into a **decision matrix based on the research constructs** (interest, autonomy, meaning, management aspiration, pay, stress, regret risk).

answer:
Yes. The literature does not usually ask “interesting job vs higher-paid less-interesting job” in exactly those words, but it has studied the underlying pieces for decades:

- **intrinsic job characteristics**: interestingness, challenge, skill variety, autonomy, learning
- **meaningful work**
- **person-job / person-organization fit**
- **pay**
- **promotion / status**
- **overall well-being, job satisfaction, turnover, burnout**

## Short version

Across organizational psychology and well-being research, the pattern is fairly consistent:

1. **Doing work you find intrinsically interesting/meaningful is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and engagement.**
2. **Pay matters, but its relationship with job satisfaction is much weaker than most people intuitively expect.**
3. **Extra income improves life evaluation more reliably than day-to-day emotional well-being, and the gains tend to diminish as income rises.**
4. **Fit matters a lot**: people are happier when the job matches their interests, values, and preferred way of working.
5. **Direct evidence on “regret” is thinner**, but broader regret research suggests people often regret **not pursuing identity-relevant goals** more than making an imperfect choice.

So if by “interesting” you mean “I would actually enjoy the day-to-day work,” the research generally says that matters a lot for sustained well-being.

---

## 1) “Interesting work” is strongly linked to satisfaction and well-being

### Job design research
A classic and still influential line of research is the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham), later updated by large meta-analyses.

A major meta-analysis:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**  
  reviewed a very large work-design literature and found that characteristics like:
  - **autonomy**
  - **task significance**
  - **skill variety**
  - **feedback**
  
  were consistently associated with:
  - higher **job satisfaction**
  - higher **internal work motivation**
  - better **performance**
  - lower **strain/burnout-related outcomes**

This is one of the strongest bodies of evidence relevant to your question. In plain English:  
**jobs that are more engaging and inherently rewarding tend to produce better subjective outcomes than jobs that are merely better compensated.**

### Meaningful work
Another large literature studies **meaningful work**, which overlaps with “interesting” but is not identical.

- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)**, meta-analysis on meaningful work  
  found that meaningful work is associated with:
  - higher **job satisfaction**
  - higher **work engagement**
  - higher **life satisfaction**
  - stronger **organizational commitment**
  - lower **turnover intentions**
  - lower **burnout**

The effect sizes are generally **moderate**, not tiny. So this is not a trivial factor.

A useful summary statement from this literature would be:

> On average, people in work they experience as meaningful or intrinsically engaging report substantially better work-related well-being than people in less meaningful work.

---

## 2) Pay matters, but much less strongly than people often assume

The best-known result here:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**, meta-analysis  
  found the relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but modest**.  
  The commonly cited overall correlation is around **r ≈ .15**.

That is not zero. Money matters. But it is **far weaker** than many people expect.

So if your question is:

> “If I take the more interesting job but give up some salary, am I likely to be less happy?”

the average research answer is:

- **not necessarily**
- and often **no**, especially if the salary difference is not changing your financial security in a major way

### Income and overall happiness
On broader well-being, the famous papers are:

- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)**  
  found income strongly related to **life evaluation** (“How is your life going overall?”), but less so to **daily emotional well-being**, with evidence of a plateau in their data.

- **Killingsworth (2021)** and **Kahneman, Killingsworth, & Mellers (2023)**  
  later refined this and argued that well-being can continue to rise with income for many people, but the relationship is still **nonlinear** and depends on baseline well-being.

The practical takeaway is:

- More money usually helps.
- But beyond a comfortable level, it tends to buy **smaller increments** in well-being.
- Those gains show up more in **status/life evaluation** than in the felt quality of your everyday work.

That distinction matters for your case:
- a higher salary and leadership title may improve “how successful my career looks”
- interesting work may contribute more to “how much I enjoy my Tuesdays”

Research often finds people underweight that second part.

---

## 3) Person-job fit is a major predictor of satisfaction

Another very relevant literature is **person-environment fit**.

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**, meta-analysis  
  found that:
  - **person-job fit**
  - **person-organization fit**
  
  are strongly associated with:
  - higher **job satisfaction**
  - higher **organizational commitment**
  - lower **intentions to quit**

This is important because there is no universal answer to “should I optimize for interesting work or leadership opportunity?”  
The research says the better predictor is often:

> Which role fits your motives, values, skills, and preferred way of working?

If “building/doing research” is your strongest intrinsic driver, that matters.  
If “leading people and having broader impact through others” is genuinely a core motivation, that matters too.

---

## 4) Job satisfaction does spill into overall life satisfaction

Your question is about being “happier,” not just liking work more.

The research generally finds that **job satisfaction and life satisfaction are moderately related**, and the relationship appears to run in **both directions** over time.

So this is not just about whether you enjoy work in a narrow sense. A more satisfying job tends, on average, to improve overall well-being too.

This means the choice is not merely “career optimization” — it plausibly affects broader life happiness.

---

## 5) What about regret?

Here the evidence is less direct.

There is **much less research specifically on “career regret after choosing a more interesting vs better-paid job.”**  
Most occupational studies measure:
- job satisfaction
- commitment
- burnout
- turnover intention
- career success
rather than regret per se.

### What broader regret research says
The classic finding from regret research is:

- **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)**  
  Over time, people tend to regret **inactions** more than **actions**.

That does **not** mean “always take the new offer.”  
But it does suggest something relevant to your case:

- If **leading a team** is something you have wanted for a long time and see as identity-relevant,
- then **never testing that path** may be a source of later regret.

At the same time, research on fit and meaningful work suggests:
- taking a role that is misaligned with your intrinsic interests can reduce daily well-being even if it looks like advancement.

So the regret literature does **not** override the meaningful-work literature. It just says:
- long-term regret often centers on **untried selves** and **missed identity goals**.

---

## 6) A subtle but important finding: people often mispredict what will matter

Research on **affective forecasting** and the **focusing illusion** suggests people often overestimate the happiness impact of salient factors like:
- salary
- prestige
- title
- brand name

and underestimate the impact of:
- daily task content
- autonomy
- who they work with
- how they spend their hours

This is highly relevant to job decisions.

In other words, people are often tempted to optimize for the parts of the decision that are easiest to narrate socially, rather than the parts that most affect lived experience.

That is not my opinion; it is a robust theme in behavioral decision research.

---

## 7) Important caveats

### Most of this evidence is not a perfect causal experiment
A lot of the literature is:
- cross-sectional
- longitudinal
- meta-analytic

which is strong, but not the same as randomly assigning people to jobs.

So the safest statement is:
- people in more meaningful/intrinsically rewarding jobs are **consistently more satisfied on average**
- and there is good reason to think at least part of that is causal
- but self-selection also plays a role

### “Interesting” is not one thing
A job can be:
- interesting but exhausting
- meaningful but bureaucratic
- leadership-heavy but less intellectually stimulating
- autonomous but lonely

The literature suggests the best outcomes come from combinations like:
- **interest + autonomy + fit + manageable demands + good relationships**

not from “interestingness” alone.

### Meaning can have a dark side
There is also research showing that people who feel called to work can tolerate:
- lower pay
- overwork
- exploitation
- burnout

So “interesting” or “meaningful” is not a magic shield if the role is chronically unhealthy.

---

## Bottom line from the research

If I reduce the literature to the most defensible conclusion, it is:

- **Yes, people who do work they find intrinsically interesting/meaningful tend to be happier at work, and usually somewhat happier overall.**
- **Pay increases happiness too, but the effect on job satisfaction is much smaller than the effect of intrinsic job quality and fit.**
- **There is not strong evidence that people systematically regret choosing the more interesting job over the better-paid one.**
- **If anything, the stronger evidence is that misfit and low intrinsic interest predict dissatisfaction, burnout, and wanting to leave.**
- **The main regret-related risk in your case is not “choosing interesting work”; it is possibly not trying a long-desired leadership path if that is central to your identity.**

So the research would push you to ask not “which package is objectively better?” but:

1. Which role gives me better **day-to-day intrinsic experience**?
2. Which role better fits my **core motives**:
   - research depth?
   - leadership/impact-through-others?
3. Is the salary difference large enough to materially change life quality, or is it mostly symbolic?
4. Will I regret not trying management because it represents an important possible self?

If you want, I can give you a **research-based decision framework** for this exact choice, with weights grounded in the literature rather than personal advice.

Yes — there is a fair amount of research on this, and the broad result is:

**Intrinsic qualities of work** — how interesting, meaningful, autonomous, and growth-producing the work feels — are usually **stronger and more durable predictors of job satisfaction and well-being than modest differences in pay**.

That’s the short version. The longer, more careful version is below.

## 1) What the research says, in broad strokes

### A. “Interesting work” matters a lot
In organizational psychology, this usually gets measured indirectly through things like:

- **skill variety**: do you use different abilities?
- **task significance**: does the work matter?
- **task identity**: do you own meaningful chunks of work?
- **autonomy**: do you control how you work?
- **feedback**: do you learn how well you’re doing?

This goes back to the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham). Across decades of studies, jobs with these characteristics tend to produce:

- higher **job satisfaction**
- higher **internal motivation**
- higher **engagement**
- often better **well-being**

A major meta-analysis by **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** found that work-design characteristics like autonomy, skill variety, task significance, etc. have **moderate-to-strong associations** with job satisfaction and related outcomes.

### B. Pay matters, but usually less than people expect
Pay does affect satisfaction. But in most research on job satisfaction:

- **pay is not irrelevant**
- yet **it is often not the strongest predictor**
- after a reasonable standard of living is achieved, **job content and work environment often matter more for day-to-day well-being**

Economists and psychologists both find this in somewhat different ways:

- **Job satisfaction studies** usually show that intrinsic job characteristics are stronger predictors than salary alone.
- **Labor economics** has the idea of **compensating differentials**: people often accept lower pay for better non-monetary job attributes (more pleasant, meaningful, flexible, interesting work). That itself is evidence that workers value those attributes a lot.

So if one job is “a bit higher salary” but the other is much more intrinsically rewarding, the literature would generally say: **don’t treat the salary difference as the main variable** unless it’s financially meaningful for your life.

### C. Meaningful work is strongly linked to well-being
A related literature looks at **meaningful work** or **calling**. The general finding is that people who experience their work as meaningful report:

- higher job satisfaction
- higher engagement
- higher sense of purpose
- often better overall well-being

There are reviews and meta-analyses on this theme as well. The effect is not trivial.

But one caution from that same literature:
- meaningful work can also make people tolerate overwork or exploitation more than they should.

So “interesting” is good, but **interesting + unsustainable** is not automatically better.

### D. Autonomy/control is especially important
One of the most consistent findings across occupational health research is that **control over one’s work** is a major driver of well-being.

Jobs with low control and high demands are reliably worse for mental health. Models like **Karasek’s demand-control model** and later work in occupational health support this.

If the more interesting job also gives you more real autonomy, that is a meaningful plus.

---

## 2) Do people who choose the more interesting job tend to be happier?

**On average, yes — if “interesting” means intrinsically motivating, meaningful, and autonomous.**

But with an important caveat:

What predicts happiness is not “interestingness” in the abstract. It’s usually a bundle of things:

- interesting/challenging tasks
- autonomy
- sense of progress
- sense of impact
- fit with personal identity
- good colleagues/manager
- manageable workload

So the research supports something like:

> People are typically happier in jobs that satisfy intrinsic psychological needs and provide meaningful, engaging work, even if another option pays somewhat more.

That’s a strong generalization from the literature.

---

## 3) Do people regret staying in the more interesting job?

Here the evidence is weaker.

There is **much less direct research** on:
- “I chose the more interesting technical role over the better-paid leadership role”
- “Did I regret it 5–10 years later?”

What the literature does support more clearly is:

### A. Better intrinsic fit predicts lower turnover intentions and higher satisfaction
If a role fits your interests/identity/strengths better, people tend to:
- want to leave less
- feel more satisfied
- feel more committed

This is the **person-job fit / person-organization fit** literature.

### B. Promotions and extrinsic gains often show adaptation
There is research suggesting that promotions and raises can improve satisfaction, but often:
- the boost is **real but temporary**
- people **adapt**
- workload and stress may rise

In some longitudinal work on promotions, people get an initial increase in job satisfaction, but the effect can fade, while hours/stress may persist.

So if the choice is:
- **more interesting work**
vs
- **slightly more money + title/status + more management**

the evidence suggests the second option can feel better at first, but may not produce more durable well-being unless the leadership work itself is intrinsically rewarding to you.

---

## 4) A very important point in your case: leadership is not just “extrinsic”

Your description actually includes **two different kinds of intrinsic rewards**:

1. **Google role**: more interesting work content  
2. **OpenAI role**: leading a small team, which you say you’ve always wanted

That matters a lot.

From the research perspective, “leading a small team” is not just pay/status. It may satisfy:

- **autonomy**
- **impact**
- **growth/mastery**
- **identity**
- **meaning**
- **relatedness**

So the evidence does **not** cleanly reduce to:
> “interesting work vs money”

It may instead be:
> “interesting technical work vs less interesting technical work but more meaningful leadership growth”

And research would say: both are intrinsic values; the key question is **which one is more central to your well-being and identity**.

---

## 5) What factors tend to matter most, according to the literature

If I compress the evidence into a rough hierarchy for well-being/job satisfaction, it would look something like this:

### Usually very important
- **autonomy/control**
- **interesting/challenging work**
- **sense of meaning/impact**
- **fit with your strengths and identity**
- **quality of manager and team**
- **sustainable workload**

### Important, but often less decisive than above once pay is already good
- absolute salary
- title
- prestige

### Mixed / highly person-dependent
- management responsibilities
- speed of career advancement
- organizational mission vs day-to-day work

That last point matters because many people overestimate the importance of mission/prestige and underestimate the importance of:
- what they actually do all day
- who they work with
- how much control they have

---

## 6) The best-supported answer to your specific question

If your question is:

> Is there research suggesting that staying in a more interesting job tends to make people happier than taking a somewhat better-paid but less interesting one?

**Yes, broadly yes.**  
The strongest evidence base in work psychology says that **intrinsic job quality** — interestingness, autonomy, meaningfulness, skill use — is a major and often stronger predictor of job satisfaction and well-being than moderate pay differences.

If your question is:

> Do people later regret prioritizing interesting work?

**There is much less direct evidence on regret specifically.**  
But what evidence exists on long-term satisfaction points more toward:
- **intrinsic fit** being durable
- **extrinsic gains** producing more adaptation

So the literature leans in favor of **interesting/meaningful work**, unless the alternative also provides a different kind of intrinsic reward that matters deeply to you — such as leading a team.

---

## 7) Good papers / research traditions to look at

A few places to start:

### Work design / interestingness
- **Hackman & Oldham (1976, 1980)** — Job Characteristics Model  
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — meta-analysis of work design and outcomes

### Well-being at work
- **Warr** — work, happiness, and mental health; “vitamin model”
- **Karasek** — demand-control model
- **Bakker & Demerouti** — Job Demands-Resources model

### Intrinsic motivation / psychological needs
- **Deci & Ryan** — Self-Determination Theory
- Need satisfaction at work: autonomy, competence, relatedness

### Job satisfaction and pay
- **Andrew Clark** and related labor-economics work on job satisfaction
- literature on **compensating wage differentials**

### Promotion / adaptation
- longitudinal studies on promotion and job satisfaction, e.g. **Georgellis, Lange, & Tabvuma** and related work

### Meaningful work
- reviews/meta-analyses by authors such as **Rosso**, **Allan**, **Steger**, **Duffy**, etc.

---

## 8) One evidence-based way to frame your decision

Based on the literature, the highest-value questions are probably not:

- “Which one pays more?”
- “Which company is more prestigious?”

But rather:

1. **Which role gives me more autonomy/control day to day?**
2. **Which role will feel more engaging at 10 a.m. on a random Tuesday?**
3. **Does leading a small team feel intrinsically meaningful to me, or mainly like status?**
4. **Which role better fits who I want to become over the next 3–5 years?**
5. **What is the workload/stress tradeoff?**
6. **What is the quality of the manager and immediate team?**

That is much closer to how the evidence would tell you to think about it.

If you want, I can also give you:
- a **short annotated bibliography** of the strongest papers/meta-analyses, or
- a **decision framework grounded in the research** rather than opinion.

answer:
Yes. The broad research result is:

**Interesting, meaningful, and autonomous work is usually a stronger predictor of job satisfaction and day-to-day well-being than pay differences, especially once pay is already high.**  
But there’s an important complication in your case: **“leading a small team” is not just an extrinsic perk**. If leadership is something you genuinely want, that can itself be an intrinsic source of satisfaction.

## What the literature says

### 1) “Interesting work” is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction
A large literature in organizational psychology finds that jobs are more satisfying when they provide:

- **skill variety / challenge**
- **task significance / meaning**
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**
- **opportunities for growth**

This goes back to the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham), and later meta-analyses found these factors have **moderate, reliable associations** with job satisfaction, motivation, and engagement.

A key review/meta-analysis:
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, *Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features...*  
  Their summary was basically: jobs that are more enriching and less deadening tend to produce more satisfaction and internal motivation.

So on the narrow question “does doing more interesting work tend to make people happier at work?” — **yes, strongly, on average**.

---

### 2) Meaningful work is linked not just to job satisfaction, but to broader well-being
More recent research on **meaningful work** finds that people who experience their work as meaningful tend to report:

- higher job satisfaction
- higher work engagement
- better general well-being / life satisfaction
- lower turnover intentions
- lower burnout

Meta-analytic work by **Bryan Allan and colleagues** and related reviews points in this direction quite consistently.

So if by “interesting” you also mean “I care about the substance of the work,” not just “it’s cognitively fun,” that tends to matter a lot.

---

### 3) Pay matters, but less than many people expect
There are two separate literatures here:

#### a) Pay and job satisfaction
A meta-analysis by **Judge et al. (2010)** found the relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but modest**. In plain English:

- more pay helps
- but the effect is not huge
- and it is generally **weaker than intrinsic job characteristics**

Also, **pay fairness** often matters more than absolute pay. Going from “underpaid” to “fairly paid” matters a lot. Going from “very well paid” to “even better paid” matters much less.

#### b) Income and overall happiness
The income/well-being literature also shows **diminishing returns**. Exact interpretations differ across studies, but the robust part is:

- extra income helps more at lower income levels
- once someone is already financially comfortable, marginal gains in well-being from additional income are smaller

Given the jobs you mention, this is especially relevant: a “bit higher salary” at already-high compensation levels is likely to have **much less impact on well-being** than a large difference in day-to-day work quality.

---

### 4) Person–job fit matters a lot
This may be the most important nuance for your case.

Research on **person-job fit** and **person-organization fit** finds that satisfaction is highest when the role matches what the person values, enjoys, and wants to become.

A major meta-analysis:
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**

This matters because your tradeoff is not simply:

- interesting work **vs**
- more money

It is also:

- interesting technical/research work **vs**
- a leadership role you’ve wanted for a long time

If leading a team is genuinely part of your ideal career, then that role may satisfy important intrinsic motives too:
- autonomy
- responsibility
- identity
- growth
- status/impact in a nontrivial sense

So the literature would not say “always choose the more technically interesting job.”  
It would say: **choose the role with the better overall intrinsic fit to your motives and values.**

---

### 5) Self-determination theory: autonomy, competence, and relatedness are central
A very influential framework in psychology is **Self-Determination Theory** (Deci & Ryan).

It says well-being is strongly supported when work satisfies needs for:

- **autonomy** — I have agency
- **competence** — I’m growing and effective
- **relatedness** — I feel connected to others / part of something

This is highly relevant to your situation:

- Your current role may score higher on **competence/interest**
- The proposed role may score higher on **autonomy/leadership/impact**
- Either could be better depending on which needs are underfed right now

Research in workplace settings generally supports the idea that jobs satisfying these needs are linked to higher motivation and well-being.

---

### 6) Regret research is less direct, but still informative
Here the evidence is less clean than the job-satisfaction literature.

What’s fairly robust:

- Over the long term, people often regret **missed opportunities** more than actions taken  
  (classic regret work by **Gilovich & Medvec**)
- Career/education are among the major domains of life regret  
  (e.g. **Roese & Summerville, 2005**)

But there is **not**, as far as I’d characterize it, a strong simple finding like:
> “People who choose interesting work over money regret it less”

That exact question is not as well studied.

The closest evidence suggests:
- long-run regret often centers on **failing to pursue growth, identity, or meaning**
- not merely on failing to maximize compensation

So regret research slightly favors **authentic aspiration and meaningful fit** over purely extrinsic optimization, but the evidence here is much less direct.

---

## If I compress the evidence into a few practical conclusions

### What predicts happiness at work most reliably?
Usually:
1. **Interesting / meaningful work**
2. **Autonomy**
3. **Good colleagues / manager / culture**
4. **Fit with your values and identity**
5. **Growth and mastery**
6. **Fair pay**

Not usually:
- a modest salary increase by itself, especially at already high income

---

## What the research would suggest about your specific tradeoff

If the choice were only:

- **more interesting work at Google**
vs
- **slightly more money at OpenAI**

then the evidence would lean pretty clearly toward **the more interesting work** as the better bet for long-term satisfaction.

But your actual choice is:

- **interesting technical research**
vs
- **less interesting content, but first real leadership role**

Research would frame that as:
- not “intrinsic vs extrinsic”
- but **one form of intrinsic reward vs another**

If “leading a small team” is something you deeply want, that can matter as much as technical interest.

---

## The strongest evidence-backed summary

If you want the shortest evidence-based answer:

- **Yes, people in more interesting/meaningful jobs tend to be happier at work.**
- **Yes, those job features usually matter more than modest pay differences.**
- **No, the research does not strongly say that choosing the more interesting job always leads to less regret.**
- **What predicts satisfaction best is overall fit: interestingness, meaning, autonomy, growth, and whether the role matches the kind of person you want to become.**

---

## References worth looking at
A few especially relevant ones:

- **Hackman, J. R., & Oldham, G. R.** — Job Characteristics Model
- **Humphrey, S. E., Nahrgang, J. D., & Morgeson, F. P. (2007)** — Meta-analysis on work design
- **Judge, T. A., Piccolo, R. F., Podsakoff, N. P., Shaw, J. C., & Rich, B. L. (2010)** — Meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction
- **Kristof-Brown, A. L., Zimmerman, R. D., & Johnson, E. C. (2005)** — Meta-analysis on person-environment fit
- **Deci, E. L., & Ryan, R. M.** / **Gagné & Deci (2005)** — Self-Determination Theory at work
- **Allan, B. A., et al.** — Meta-analytic work on meaningful work and well-being
- **Gilovich, T., & Medvec, V. H.** — Long-term regret tends to shift toward inaction
- **Roese, N. J., & Summerville, A. (2005)** — Domains of regret

If you want, I can turn this into a **very compact “research-weighted decision framework”** for your exact choice, without giving personal-opinion advice.

**Summarizing meaningful work outcomes**
