answer:
Yes — there is a fairly strong research literature suggesting that **interesting/meaningful work tends to matter a lot for well-being**, often **more than salary differences**, and that **fit with what you personally value** matters a great deal.

The short version:

- **Interesting, meaningful, autonomous work** is one of the strongest and most consistent predictors of **job satisfaction** and lower burnout.
- **Pay matters**, but its relationship to job satisfaction is usually **much smaller** than people expect.
- **Leadership/promotion** can improve satisfaction, but the evidence is more mixed: it often brings more stress and only helps clearly when it matches a person’s motives and values.
- There is **less direct research on regret** for this exact choice, but the closest literature suggests people often regret **not pursuing identity-relevant goals**.

## 1) “Interesting work” is strongly linked to job satisfaction

A lot of this comes from the **job design** literature.

### Classic finding: the nature of the work itself matters a lot
The classic framework is **Hackman & Oldham’s Job Characteristics Model**. It says work is more motivating and satisfying when it has:

- **skill variety**
- **task identity**
- **task significance**
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**

These are basically close cousins of what people mean by “interesting work.”

### Meta-analytic evidence
A major meta-analysis is:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, *Journal of Applied Psychology*

They reviewed a very large literature on work design and found that “motivating” job characteristics — especially **autonomy**, **variety**, **significance**, and **feedback** — are **robustly associated with job satisfaction, internal motivation, performance, and lower strain**.

The broad takeaway from that literature is:

- People in jobs with richer, more engaging task structure are, on average, **substantially more satisfied**.
- These effects are not tiny. They are usually **moderate** in size and much larger than the effect of salary level alone.

So if by “more interesting” you mean **more intellectually engaging, more varied, more meaningful, more like research than management overhead**, the literature generally says that should predict **higher job satisfaction**.

## 2) Meaningful work also predicts broader well-being, not just “I like my job”

There is a related literature on **meaningful work**.

A useful synthesis is:

- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)**, meta-analysis on outcomes of meaningful work

Their conclusion was that perceived meaningful work is associated with:

- higher **job satisfaction**
- higher **work engagement**
- stronger **organizational commitment**
- lower **turnover intentions**
- better **overall well-being** / life satisfaction, though the effect on overall life satisfaction is usually smaller than on job satisfaction

That distinction matters:

- **Interesting/meaningful work strongly affects how you feel about work**
- It also affects **general well-being**, but less strongly, because life satisfaction depends on many non-work factors too

So the evidence is strongest for “you’ll like your job more,” and moderately supportive for “you’ll be happier overall.”

## 3) Salary matters, but usually less than people think

The relevant result here is surprisingly consistent:

### Pay level has only a small relationship with job satisfaction
A widely cited meta-analysis is:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**, meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction

The key result: **the correlation between how much people are paid and how satisfied they are with their job is positive but small**.

A good plain-English summary is:

- More pay helps
- But the effect is **much weaker** than the effect of job quality, fit, autonomy, meaning, or manager quality
- **Pay fairness** and **relative pay** often matter more psychologically than absolute pay

This is especially relevant in your case because you’re already talking about two **very high-end jobs**. Research generally finds that once basic and comfort needs are well met, **marginal salary differences have diminishing impact on experienced well-being**.

So, if the OpenAI offer is only “a bit higher salary,” the research would usually say:

> the salary bump is real, but it is probably not the main determinant of long-run satisfaction

## 4) Leadership opportunities can matter a lot — if they fit your motives

This is where your situation is more nuanced than “interesting job vs money.”

You said:

> leading a small team is something I always wanted to do

That matters, and the literature would treat it as more than a superficial perk.

### Person-job fit research
A major meta-analysis here is:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** on person-job/person-organization fit

The general result: **fit between the person and the role** is strongly associated with:

- higher job satisfaction
- stronger commitment
- lower intentions to quit
- better well-being at work

So if leadership is a genuine long-term aspiration for you, then a role that gives you that may improve well-being **even if the day-to-day work is less inherently interesting**.

In other words, the literature would not treat “interesting research” as the only intrinsic good. It would also treat:

- leadership
- influence
- responsibility
- development toward a desired identity

as psychologically meaningful job attributes.

### Promotions/management are mixed on average
The research on promotions and moving into management is less clean-cut than the research on interesting work.

What tends to show up in longitudinal studies:

- Promotions often produce an **increase in job satisfaction**, but not always a huge one
- The gains may be **temporary**
- They often come with **more stress, longer hours, more coordination work, and less time doing the task itself**
- Benefits are larger when the new role brings **more autonomy and fits the person’s motives**

So the evidence is **not** “management makes people happier.”
It is closer to:

> management helps when it matches what the person wants, but it often disappoints people who mainly liked the craft itself

That seems especially relevant for researchers becoming managers.

## 5) Self-determination theory: autonomy, competence, relatedness

Another important literature is **Self-Determination Theory**.

The basic claim is that well-being at work improves when work satisfies needs for:

- **autonomy** — I have real control
- **competence** — I’m using and growing my abilities
- **relatedness** — I feel connected/respected

Across many studies, work that supports these needs predicts:

- greater motivation
- higher satisfaction
- lower burnout
- better psychological well-being

This is useful because it helps unpack your tradeoff:

- Your Google role may score high on **competence** and maybe autonomy through interesting research
- The OpenAI role may score high on **autonomy/influence** and perhaps relatedness if leading a team is meaningful to you

So from a research standpoint, the real question is not just “interesting vs less interesting,” but:

> which role better satisfies your key psychological needs?

## 6) On regret: evidence is weaker, but there are suggestive findings

There is **less direct evidence** on “Do people regret choosing the more interesting job over the higher-paid, higher-status one?”

I’m not aware of a strong body of research that answers that exact question cleanly.

But there are two nearby findings:

### A) People often regret inaction on important aspirations
The general regret literature (e.g. **Gilovich & Medvec**) finds that over time people often regret **not pursuing important possibilities** more than they regret actions that turned out imperfectly.

That is not job-specific evidence, but it suggests:

- if “leading a team” is a real aspiration, not just an ego boost, passing on it could become regret-prone
- likewise, if “doing genuinely interesting research” is central to your identity, giving that up could also become regret-prone

So regret research doesn’t say “choose interesting work.”
It says more:

> long-term regrets often cluster around abandoning core parts of one’s identity or ideals

### B) People adapt to extrinsic gains
A lot of well-being research suggests people adapt fairly quickly to improvements in:
- income
- status
- titles

They adapt less fully to the **day-to-day quality of lived experience**.

That favors giving serious weight to:
- what the work feels like every day
- whether you actually enjoy the tasks
- whether the role fits who you want to become

## 7) If I translate the literature into your exact tradeoff

The research would probably say:

### Strongly supported
- If one job is **meaningfully more interesting, autonomous, and engaging**, it is likely to produce **higher job satisfaction**
- A **small salary increase** by itself is unlikely to compensate much for a worse day-to-day work experience

### Also strongly supported
- If the less interesting job better fits an important aspiration of yours — like **wanting to lead** — that can matter a lot, because **fit** is a major determinant of satisfaction

### Mixed / uncertain
- Whether management will make *you* happier depends on whether you truly enjoy:
  - coaching
  - hiring
  - alignment
  - performance management
  - owning outcomes through others  
  rather than mainly enjoying being a strong individual contributor

That last point is important because many people say they want to “lead,” but what they really want is:
- more influence
- more agency
- more scope

Those are not the same as enjoying management.

Research on management transitions suggests those distinctions matter.

## 8) A fair summary of the evidence

If I had to compress the literature into one sentence:

> For long-run work happiness, intrinsic job quality (interestingness, meaning, autonomy, fit) usually matters more than modest pay differences; leadership opportunities help mainly when they genuinely match the person’s values and motives.

Or even more bluntly:

- **Interesting work usually beats a small pay bump**
- **A desired leadership path can beat interesting work if leadership is truly part of your ideal career**
- The biggest risk is choosing based on **prestige or money** while ignoring the day-to-day experience of the role

## 9) Papers/literatures worth looking at

If you want to read the actual research, I’d start with these:

- **Hackman & Oldham (1976)** — Job Characteristics Model  
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — meta-analysis of work design
- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)** — meta-analysis of meaningful work
- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)** — meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** — meta-analysis on person-job / person-organization fit
- Broader **Self-Determination Theory at work** literature on autonomy, competence, and relatedness

If you want, I can do one of two follow-ups:

1. give you a **more formal mini-literature review with citations and key effect sizes**, or  
2. turn this into a **research-based decision matrix** specifically for your Google vs OpenAI choice.

answer:
Yes—there is a fairly large literature, and the broad answer is:

**“Interesting” work (more precisely: intrinsically motivating, meaningful, autonomy-rich, growth-promoting, and fitting your interests) is usually one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and work-related well-being.**  
By comparison, **pay matters, but typically less than people expect**, especially once compensation is already high enough that basic security and lifestyle are not at issue.

A few important nuances:

- Research is **strongest for job satisfaction and engagement**, somewhat weaker but still positive for **overall life satisfaction**.
- There is **less direct research on “regret after choosing the more/less interesting job”** specifically.
- “Interesting” work is not everything: bad manager, overload, politics, or low autonomy can erase its benefits.

## What the research most consistently finds

### 1) Intrinsic job quality is a major driver of well-being
A large body of organizational psychology shows that people are happier at work when jobs provide:

- **variety**
- **autonomy**
- **task significance / meaning**
- **opportunities to learn**
- **feedback**
- **fit with their interests/strengths**

This comes from the classic **Job Characteristics Model** and later meta-analytic work.

**Key result:** these features are usually linked to **substantially higher job satisfaction, motivation, engagement, and lower burnout/turnover intentions**.

Relevant research:
- **Hackman & Oldham** — Job Characteristics Model
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — large meta-analysis of work design

The Humphrey et al. meta-analysis is especially relevant: it found that motivational aspects of work design—especially **autonomy** and **meaningfulness-related features**—have robust relationships with job satisfaction and internal motivation.

### 2) Meaningful work is strongly associated with satisfaction and well-being
There is also a more recent literature on **meaningful work**.

**General finding:** people who experience their work as meaningful report:
- higher **job satisfaction**
- higher **work engagement**
- better **overall well-being**
- less **psychological distress**

Relevant review/meta-analysis:
- **Allan et al. (2019)** — meta-analysis on antecedents and outcomes of meaningful work

This literature suggests that “interesting” work often helps because it is not only stimulating but also easier to experience as **personally significant**.

### 3) Person-job fit matters a lot
Another robust line of research is **person-job fit** and **interest congruence**.

If your work fits your interests, values, and abilities, you tend to have:
- higher job satisfaction
- better commitment
- lower intention to leave
- often better performance

Relevant research:
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** — meta-analysis on fit at work
- Holland-type vocational fit research also points in the same direction

This is probably one of the closest research proxies to your specific question. If the Google role is much more interesting to you, it may simply be a **better fit**, and fit has fairly consistent benefits.

### 4) Pay matters, but less strongly than intrinsic aspects of work
There is a common intuition that higher pay should strongly raise job satisfaction. In the literature, the relationship exists—but is often **smaller than people expect**.

A widely cited meta-analytic conclusion is that the correlation between **pay level and job satisfaction is positive but modest**—much weaker than the correlations typically seen for things like:
- meaningfulness
- autonomy
- good management
- job fit
- social climate

Relevant research:
- **Judge et al.** meta-analytic work on pay and job satisfaction
- broader labor-econ and org psych research on job quality

So if the choice is between:

- **more interesting work + already very good salary**
vs
- **less interesting work + somewhat higher salary**

the literature generally suggests that the **interest/intrinsic-quality side often has the bigger effect on day-to-day satisfaction**.

## What about overall happiness, not just job satisfaction?

The research is a bit more mixed here, because life satisfaction depends on many things outside work. But there are good reasons to think work quality matters a lot:

1. **You spend a huge share of waking life at work.**
2. Daily experiences at work spill over into non-work mood and energy.
3. Meaningful, autonomy-supportive work supports broader psychological needs.

This aligns with **Self-Determination Theory**:

- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

When work supports these needs, people tend to do better psychologically.

Relevant review:
- **Gagné & Deci (2005)** — Self-Determination Theory and work motivation

So the literature would say: if a job is more interesting because it gives you more learning, mastery, autonomy, and meaning, that is not a trivial preference—it is tied to basic psychological needs.

## Do people regret choosing money over interesting work?

This exact question has **less direct evidence** than the points above. I’d phrase the research-based answer like this:

### What we know
- People often **mispredict** what will make them happier.
- They tend to **overweight salient extrinsic factors** like pay, status, and prestige at decision time.
- They often **underweight the quality of everyday experience**.

This is related to work on:
- **affective forecasting errors**
- the **focusing illusion**
- adaptation to extrinsic rewards

So while I don’t know of a definitive study saying “people who choose the more interesting job regret less,” the broader behavioral literature suggests that people can make choices that look better on paper but produce worse daily experience.

### Why this matters in your case
Salary is often experienced as a **less frequent evaluative reward** (“I’m paid well”), while work interest is a **daily repeated experience** (“I spend all day doing something engaging / boring”).

That repeated daily exposure is one reason intrinsic job quality can matter so much.

## A subtle point: leadership may itself be an intrinsic benefit
Your case isn’t just “interest vs salary.” It’s also:

- **more interesting individual-contributor/research work**
vs
- **less interesting work, but with a first leadership role you’ve wanted**

Research would treat leadership opportunity as a potential gain in:
- autonomy
- status
- growth
- sense of impact
- competence fulfillment

So the research-based comparison is not simply:
- interestingness vs money

It may be:
- interestingness/content fit
vs
- growth/leadership aspiration + some salary

And those are both intrinsic in different ways.

That’s important because if “leading a team” is a deep, durable aspiration—not just a prestige marker—it may carry real well-being value.

## If I had to summarize the literature in one sentence

**Among people already earning plenty, the strongest predictors of being happy with a job are usually not incremental pay, but whether the work is engaging, meaningful, autonomy-rich, and a good fit for the person.**

## Practical interpretation of the evidence for your situation
Based on the research, the main questions are probably:

1. **How much less interesting is OpenAI, really?**  
   A little less interesting may not matter much; a lot less interesting probably will.

2. **Is leading a small team intrinsically important to you, or mainly symbolically important?**  
   If it strongly fulfills growth/impact motives, it may compensate for reduced task interest.

3. **How much autonomy and meaning would each role have?**  
   These are major predictors of satisfaction.

4. **How strong is the fit?**  
   “Interesting” often really means “this fits how my mind likes to work.”

5. **Is the salary difference material, or just better?**  
   Research suggests marginal increases matter less when you are already well-compensated.

## Best-supported takeaways

### Strongly supported
- Intrinsic work characteristics are major predictors of job satisfaction.
- Meaningful, autonomy-supportive, well-fitting work is associated with better well-being.
- Pay increases happiness/job satisfaction less than most people intuit, especially at higher income levels.

### Moderately supported
- These job qualities also improve broader life satisfaction, not just work satisfaction.
- People often overvalue extrinsic rewards when choosing.

### Less well supported / less directly studied
- “People who chose the more interesting of two elite jobs later regret it less.”

If you want, I can turn this into a **research-based decision framework**—for example, a weighted scorecard using factors that the literature says matter most:
- interest/fit
- autonomy
- meaning
- manager/team quality
- leadership growth
- pay/security
- likely day-to-day experience
- burnout risk

And I can also give you a short reading list of the most relevant papers.

answer:
Yes. The research literature is fairly consistent on one broad point:

**“Interesting work”**—usually studied under labels like **intrinsic motivation, task variety, autonomy, meaningfulness, and person–job fit**—is a **strong predictor of job satisfaction and work well-being**, often **stronger than salary once pay is already good/fair**.

A few important nuances:

- Research usually does **not** use the word *interesting* directly.
- It often breaks it into components:
  - **autonomy/control**
  - **variety/learning**
  - **task significance/meaning**
  - **fit with your interests and identity**
- **Leadership** is not just an extrinsic perk; if you genuinely want to lead, that is itself an **intrinsic motivation** and matters a lot.

## What the evidence generally says

### 1) Job content matters a lot for happiness at work
The classic line of research is the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham), and later meta-analyses of work design.

The recurring finding is that jobs with more:

- **skill variety**
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**
- **task significance**
- **task identity**

produce higher:

- **job satisfaction**
- **internal motivation**
- **engagement**
- lower **burnout/withdrawal**

A widely cited meta-analysis is:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — meta-analysis on work design

Their overall conclusion was that job design characteristics are robustly related to satisfaction, motivation, performance, and lower strain. In plain English: **the day-to-day nature of the work is not a minor factor; it is central**.

### 2) Salary matters, but the relationship with job satisfaction is modest
A key result here:

- **Judge et al. (2010)** — meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction

This literature generally finds that the correlation between **absolute pay** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but modest**. The usual interpretation is:

- Pay matters
- But **not nearly as much as people often assume**
- And beyond a certain point, **fairness, relative pay, and financial security** matter more than additional dollars

So if both options already pay very well, the literature would generally predict that **differences in job quality may matter more for happiness than a moderate pay increase**.

### 3) Meaningful/interesting work has a strong association with well-being
There is also a large literature on **meaningful work**.

A representative source:

- **Allan et al.** — meta-analytic work on meaningful work and well-being

Findings are very consistent: people who experience their work as meaningful report higher:

- job satisfaction
- work engagement
- life satisfaction

and lower:

- distress
- burnout
- turnover intentions

This doesn’t mean “meaning beats everything.” It means that **meaning/interest is one of the strongest recurring predictors of good work outcomes**.

### 4) Person–job fit is crucial
Another relevant literature is **person–environment fit**, especially **person–job fit**.

Classic meta-analysis:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**

Finding: when the job matches a person’s interests, values, and strengths, people tend to show higher:

- satisfaction
- commitment
- performance
- lower intention to quit

This is especially relevant to your case, because your decision is not just:

- interesting work vs more money

It is also:

- technical/research fit vs leadership/management fit

If you have long wanted to lead a small team, that may be a real part of your “fit,” not just a résumé consideration.

### 5) Autonomy and control are especially important
There is a long occupational health literature showing that **job control/autonomy** is a major predictor of well-being and stress.

This matters because leadership roles often increase:

- control over priorities
- decision latitude
- influence

But they may also increase:

- interruptions
- people problems
- administrative load
- role conflict
- responsibility stress

So research would predict that a leadership role can improve satisfaction **if** the autonomy/influence is something you value, but can reduce satisfaction if it mainly pulls you away from the work you actually enjoy.

## On “regret”: the evidence is thinner

Here the literature is much less direct.

I don’t know of a strong body of research asking exactly:

> “If someone stays in a more interesting job rather than taking a higher-paid but less interesting role, do they later regret it?”

But there are two adjacent literatures that are relevant.

### A) Long-run regret often centers on the “ideal self”
Regret research suggests that over time, people disproportionately regret failures to pursue what felt important to their authentic or “ideal” self.

Relevant names:

- **Gilovich & Medvec** — action vs inaction regret
- **Roese & Summerville (2005)** — life domains of regret

The broad pattern is that long-run regret often comes from **not pursuing meaningful possibilities**, especially tied to growth, identity, and aspirations.

Applied cautiously to your case:

- If **leading people** is something you’ve “always wanted,” declining it may be a source of future regret.
- If **doing deeply interesting research** is central to your identity, giving it up may also be a source of future regret.

Regret research does **not** tell you which of these dominates for you. It says that **identity-congruent motives matter a lot in long-term retrospection**.

### B) People mispredict how much money/status will matter
Behavioral research on affective forecasting suggests people often **overestimate** the lasting happiness impact of prestige, salary, and some external markers.

That does **not** mean money/status don’t matter. It means that people often underweight:

- daily experience
- task content
- who they work with
- how much autonomy they have

In other words: **your future happiness is often shaped more by what Tuesday feels like than by what your title says**.

## So what does the literature imply for your exact tradeoff?

If I translate the research into a prediction, it would be something like this:

### If the new role is truly “much less interesting” in day-to-day work:
then research would generally predict a **meaningful risk of lower job satisfaction**, even if pay is higher.

### If the new role satisfies a major intrinsic motive of yours—leading a team:
then that can partly or even largely offset the lower technical interest, because leadership itself can provide:

- meaning
- agency
- growth
- identity fulfillment
- impact on others

So from a research perspective, the real comparison is not:

- **interesting work** vs **money**

It is more like:

- **current intrinsic rewards**  
  (intellectual interest, flow, technical mastery, engaging work)
  
versus

- **new intrinsic rewards + extrinsic rewards**  
  (leadership, influence, growth, salary, perhaps broader impact)

That is exactly why these decisions are hard.

## Which factors usually matter most?
If I had to rank what the literature most consistently supports for job satisfaction/well-being, it would be roughly:

1. **Interesting/meaningful work**
2. **Autonomy/control**
3. **Fit with your motives/identity**
4. **Good colleagues / manager / social climate**
5. **Fair and sufficient pay**
6. Additional pay beyond that baseline

The precise order varies by study, but that’s the broad picture.

## The most research-backed summary
A fair summary of the evidence is:

- **Yes**, people in more interesting, meaningful, autonomy-rich jobs tend to be happier at work.
- **Pay helps**, but once compensation is already high and perceived as fair, it is usually a **weaker predictor** of job satisfaction than job content.
- **Regret evidence is less direct**, but long-run regret often comes from failing to pursue what fits one’s identity and aspirations.
- In your case, **leadership is not just a perk**; it is a psychologically important factor if it genuinely matters to you.

## Papers/authors worth looking up
If you want a more serious reading list, these are good starting points:

- **Hackman & Oldham** — Job Characteristics Model
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — work design meta-analysis

answer:
Yes. The research literature is fairly consistent on one big point:

**Work that is intrinsically engaging/interesting/meaningful tends to be a strong predictor of job satisfaction and well-being, often stronger than pay differences once pay is already “good enough.”**

A few nuances matter, though:

- Researchers usually don’t study “interestingness” as a single variable.  
  They study related constructs like:
  - **intrinsic motivation**
  - **meaningful work**
  - **autonomy**
  - **skill variety / learning**
  - **person–job fit**
  - **task significance**
- The evidence for **salary** is real but generally **smaller**, especially for **job satisfaction** and **day-to-day happiness**.
- In your case, **leading a team** is important, because that is not just an extrinsic perk; it may itself satisfy intrinsic motives like autonomy, impact, growth, and identity.

## What the research says

### 1) Job design / “interesting work” has a strong relationship with satisfaction

A classic line of research is the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham), later summarized in meta-analyses.

**Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, a major meta-analysis of work design research, found that features commonly associated with “interesting work” — especially:

- **autonomy**
- **task variety**
- **task significance**
- **feedback**
- **social support**

were **substantially related to job satisfaction, internal work motivation, engagement, commitment**, and lower turnover intentions.

In plain English: jobs that let people use their abilities, learn, make decisions, and feel the work matters are reliably associated with being happier at work.

A good short summary of the literature would be:

> Among job features, **autonomy and meaningful/challenging work are among the strongest non-social predictors of job satisfaction**.

### 2) Meaningful work is strongly linked to well-being

A separate literature looks at **meaningful work** directly.

Meta-analytic reviews (for example **Allan et al.**, 2019-era work on meaningful work outcomes) show that meaningful work is associated with:

- higher **job satisfaction**
- higher **work engagement**
- greater **life satisfaction**
- greater **organizational commitment**
- lower **turnover intentions**
- less **burnout**

This is important for your question, because “interesting” work and “meaningful” work are not identical, but they overlap a lot in practice. Work people find intellectually engaging and worth doing tends to be much more satisfying over time than work they see as dull but well-paid.

### 3) Person–job fit matters a lot

The **person–environment fit** literature is also relevant.

**Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** meta-analyzed fit research and found that **person–job fit** and related forms of fit were strongly related to:

- job satisfaction
- commitment
- lower intent to quit
- better performance

This means the question is not only “interesting vs higher salary,” but also:

> Which role is more aligned with your motives, identity, and strengths?

That matters because:
- if you are deeply motivated by **technical research**, a less interesting job may hurt satisfaction even if it pays more and gives title/status.
- if you have a strong desire to **lead**, then a management role may actually be *more* intrinsically satisfying despite less-interesting domain content.

### 4) Pay matters, but less than people often assume for job satisfaction

The best-known result here is **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**, a meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction.

Their conclusion was that the relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but modest**. The correlation is usually summarized as roughly **small** relative to many job-design variables.

So: higher pay does help, but not nearly as much as many people intuitively expect.

This is very relevant to your case because the offer is only **“a bit higher salary.”** Research would generally predict that a modest increase in pay is unlikely to compensate fully for a meaningful drop in day-to-day interest, *unless* the new job improves other important things like autonomy, leadership, growth, or fit.

### 5) Income has some effect on well-being, but mostly through life evaluation, not necessarily daily experience

There is broader happiness research here too.

- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** found that income was more strongly related to **life evaluation** than to **emotional well-being**, and that emotional well-being showed diminishing returns.
- Later work, especially **Killingsworth (2021)**, suggested the plateau may be less sharp than that older paper implied.

The combined takeaway is still:

> More money generally helps, but its effect on daily felt experience is limited compared with what many people expect, especially at already high income levels.

For someone already in a highly paid role at Google/OpenAI-level compensation, the literature would predict that **non-monetary job attributes** are likely to dominate the happiness equation.

### 6) Intrinsic vs extrinsic orientation

There is also research from **Self-Determination Theory** and aspiration/value studies.

A broad finding from this literature is:

- Prioritizing **intrinsic motives** (interest, growth, mastery, meaning, connection, autonomy) is associated with better well-being.
- Prioritizing **extrinsic motives** alone (money, status, image) is associated with weaker or less durable well-being gains.

At work, satisfaction of the basic psychological needs of:
- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

is consistently associated with higher motivation and well-being.

So if “more interesting work” means:
- more competence use
- more learning
- more autonomy
- more mastery

that is exactly the kind of thing the literature says tends to support sustained satisfaction.

### 7) Job satisfaction is not trivial; it spills over into health and life

This matters because sometimes people think job satisfaction is a “soft” outcome.

**Faragher, Cass, & Cooper (2005)** meta-analyzed job satisfaction and health, finding that lower job satisfaction is associated with worse:

- mental health
- burnout
- depression/anxiety
- general well-being

So if one option would make daily work substantially less engaging, the research suggests that is not just a small annoyance; it can affect broader quality of life.

---

## Do people later regret choosing the more interesting job?

Here the evidence is **weaker and less direct**.

I’m not aware of a clean, famous body of research that says:

> “People who choose interesting work over pay later regret it less”

in exactly that form.

What the research does show, indirectly, is:

- People in jobs with higher **intrinsic motivation**, **fit**, **meaning**, and **autonomy** tend to report more satisfaction and lower turnover intentions.
- People who organize their careers around **intrinsic values** tend to have better well-being than those who organize them around purely extrinsic ones.
- Pay increases tend to have **smaller and less durable effects** on satisfaction than many intrinsic features of work.

So the best evidence-based answer is:

> There is strong evidence that more interesting / meaningful / fitting work predicts greater satisfaction and well-being.  
> There is much less direct evidence on “regret,” but the indirect evidence points in the same direction.

---

## A subtlety in your case: “less interesting work” vs “leading a small team”

This is where the research becomes more specific.

You are not really deciding between:

- interesting work  
vs  
- money

You’re deciding among multiple valued job attributes:

1. **Interesting technical/research content**
2. **Higher pay**
3. **Leadership opportunity**
4. Possibly **status/prestige**
5. Possibly **future career option value**

Research suggests all of these matter, but not equally.

The two strongest predictors in your situation are probably:

- **content fit**: how much you actually enjoy the work itself
- **role fit**: whether leading people is something you genuinely want, not just something that sounds good abstractly

Because if you truly want to manage, then the OpenAI role may not actually be “less intrinsically rewarding” overall — it could be less interesting in technical content but more meaningful in responsibility/impact/identity.

That distinction matters a lot in the literature.

---

## If I had to summarize the evidence in one paragraph

At the level of research consensus:

- **Interesting, meaningful, autonomous, well-fitting work is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and sustained well-being.**
- **Pay has a positive but comparatively modest relationship with job satisfaction**, especially once compensation is already high.
- **Direct research on regret is sparse**, but the indirect evidence suggests people tend to do better when they choose work aligned with intrinsic motivation and personal fit rather than optimizing mainly for extrinsic rewards.
- In your specific case, the leadership opportunity complicates things because **management itself may be intrinsically valuable to you**, so the relevant comparison is not simply “interesting work vs money” but “research interest vs leadership fit.”

---

## Papers / literatures worth looking at

If you want to read actual sources, I’d start with these:

1. **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**  
   *Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary and theoretical extension of the work design literature.*  
   - Big meta-analysis on autonomy, task variety, significance, etc.

2. **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**  
   *The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis of the literature.*  
   - Pay matters, but modestly.

3. **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**  
   *Consequences of individuals’ fit at work: A meta-analysis of person-job, person-organization, person-group, and person-supervisor fit.*  
   - Fit strongly predicts satisfaction and retention.

4. **Faragher, Cass, & Cooper (2005)**  
   *The relationship between job satisfaction and health: A meta-analysis.*  
   - Job satisfaction has broader well-being effects.

5. **Hackman & Oldham**  
   Classic work on the **Job Characteristics Model**  
   - The foundational theory behind why interesting, autonomous work tends to be motivating and satisfying.

6. **Allan et al.** on **meaningful work**  
   - Meta-analytic work linking meaningful work to satisfaction, engagement, and lower turnover.

7. **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** and **Killingsworth (2021)**  
   - Broader income/happiness literature.

---

If you want, I can do a second pass and give you a **more rigorous “research-only decision framework”** for your exact choice — e.g. a table with predictors like **autonomy, skill use, learning, pay, leadership fit, future option value, and expected effect size on satisfaction**.

answer:
Yes. The research literature is fairly consistent on one broad point:

**Intrinsic job qualities**—interesting work, autonomy, skill use, challenge, meaning, and person-job fit—tend to predict **job satisfaction and work-related well-being more strongly and more reliably than pay differences do**, especially once pay is already high enough that basic financial stress is low.

A careful version of that claim is below.

---

## 1) What the research usually means by “interesting work”

In the literature, your “interesting” vs “less interesting” distinction is usually split into several constructs:

- **Intrinsic motivation**: the work is engaging in itself
- **Meaningful work**: it feels significant or worthwhile
- **Autonomy**: freedom in how you work
- **Skill variety / challenge / learning**
- **Person-job fit**: the role matches your interests, values, strengths

Those are not identical, but they tend to move together in knowledge work.

---

## 2) What predicts job satisfaction most strongly?

### A. Job design / intrinsic characteristics matter a lot

A large work-design literature, starting with **Hackman & Oldham** and extended by later meta-analyses, finds that jobs with more:

- autonomy
- task significance
- skill variety
- task identity
- feedback

are associated with substantially higher:

- job satisfaction
- internal motivation
- engagement
- lower withdrawal / turnover intentions

A widely cited meta-analysis is:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — meta-analysis of work design

Their overall conclusion was that the motivational features of a job are strongly related to satisfaction and motivation.

**Implication for your question:**  
If “Google = much more interesting” means more intellectually engaging work, more meaning, more skill use, or better fit, the literature would generally predict **higher day-to-day job satisfaction** from staying, all else equal.

---

### B. Meaningful work is strongly linked to well-being

Research on **meaningful work** finds robust positive associations with:

- job satisfaction
- work engagement
- life satisfaction
- psychological well-being

and negative associations with:

- burnout
- turnover intentions

A useful summary is:

- **Allan et al. (2019)** — meta-analysis on outcomes of meaningful work

The exact effect sizes vary by study, but the general pattern is not subtle: people who experience their work as meaningful tend to do better psychologically.

**Caveat:** meaningful work is not a magic shield. People sometimes tolerate poor conditions because the work feels important. But in normal comparisons between two strong jobs, meaning/interest is usually a major plus.

---

### C. Person-job fit is one of the strongest predictors

Another robust finding is that **fit** matters a lot.

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** — meta-analysis on person-environment fit

When people feel a role fits their interests and values, they report:

- higher satisfaction
- stronger commitment
- lower intention to leave

This is especially relevant in your case because the tradeoff is not just salary vs interest. It is also:

- **researcher identity** vs **manager/lead identity**
- **content fit** vs **role fit**

If leading a small team is something you’ve long wanted, that may increase fit on one dimension even if the subject matter is less interesting.

---

## 3) How important is pay, compared with interesting work?

### A. Pay matters, but less than many people expect

A frequently cited meta-analysis is:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)** — meta-analysis of pay and job satisfaction

The headline result is that the relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but modest**. The correlation is much smaller than many people intuitively expect.

A good plain-English summary is:

- Higher pay does increase satisfaction somewhat.
- But it is **not** among the strongest drivers once other job features are considered.

This is especially relevant for high-income professionals: when you are already well compensated, an extra increment of salary often has **smaller well-being returns** than changes in autonomy, meaning, interest, or fit.

---

### B. Income affects life evaluation more than day-to-day enjoyment

The broader happiness literature also matters here.

- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** found that income relates more strongly to **life evaluation** than to **daily emotional well-being**.
- Later work, especially **Killingsworth (2021)** and follow-up debates, suggests the picture is more nuanced than a simple plateau, but the broad point still stands:
  - money helps
  - the relationship is not zero
  - but **marginal gains are smaller at high income**, and money is not the dominant predictor of daily work experience

So if the salary difference is only “a bit higher,” the literature would generally predict a **smaller happiness effect** from that pay bump than from a large difference in whether the work itself is compelling.

---

## 4) What about leadership opportunities?

This is where your case gets more complicated.

You said the OpenAI role is:

- less interesting in content
- but offers a chance to **lead a small team**, which you’ve always wanted

Research suggests promotions and leadership roles are **mixed** for well-being:

- They often increase:
  - status
  - pay
  - perceived advancement
- But they can also increase:
  - stress
  - time pressure
  - role conflict
  - responsibility load

There is some evidence that promotions increase satisfaction in the short term, especially pay satisfaction, but the effect is often not huge and can fade.

A relevant paper is:

- **Kosteas (2011)** on promotions and job satisfaction

A fair summary is:

- leadership can improve satisfaction **if it matches a valued identity or growth goal**
- but leadership is not automatically better for well-being than a strong individual-contributor role

So the research would not say “take the leadership role” or “avoid it.”  
It would say: **if leading people is intrinsically meaningful to you**, then that is itself an intrinsic job quality, not merely an extrinsic perk.

---

## 5) Does more interesting work lead to less regret?

This is the weakest part of the evidence base.

I don’t know of a strong, clean literature that directly asks:

> If people choose the more interesting job over the higher-paying/promotional one, do they later regret it less?

The literature is much stronger on:

- job satisfaction
- meaning
- engagement
- turnover
- burnout
- well-being

than on explicit **career regret** in this exact tradeoff.

What we do know indirectly:

1. **Poor fit** predicts dissatisfaction and turnover.  
2. **Interesting/meaningful work** predicts stronger well-being.  
3. **Extrinsic gains** like money/status usually matter less than people forecast, especially among already advantaged groups.  
4. General regret research often finds that in the long run people regret **missed opportunities** more than actions, but that literature is broad and not specific enough to use as a strong guide here.

So I would treat “regret” evidence as **suggestive but not decisive**.

---

## 6) One important exception: if “interesting” comes with worse conditions

Interest is not everything. People are less happy when a job has:

- excessive hours
- chronic unpredictability
- poor management
- low autonomy despite exciting mission
- weak boundaries / burnout risk

Some meaningful-work research notes a dark side: people will overinvest in work they care about.

So the literature would not support a simplistic rule like:

> “Always choose the more interesting job.”

It supports something more like:

> Choose the role with the best combination of intrinsic motivation, meaning, autonomy, fit, and sustainable conditions; salary differences matter, but often less.

---

## 7) What the evidence would predict in *your* specific setup

Given your description:

- **Google**: more interesting work
- **OpenAI**: a bit higher salary, chance to lead a small team, but less interesting content

The research would roughly predict:

### If the salary difference is modest
Then the pay increase alone is **unlikely** to outweigh a substantial drop in interest/meaning, especially for someone already in a high-paying role.

### If leading a team is a deeply valued goal
Then the OpenAI role may score high on **intrinsic growth**, identity fit, and autonomy—even if the research topic itself is less compelling. That could genuinely improve satisfaction.

### The biggest unknown is role fit
The most important question from the literature is probably not:

- “interesting vs more money?”

but:

- **“Do I want to optimize for being a researcher, or for becoming a leader of researchers?”**

That distinction maps directly onto the person-job fit literature.

---

## 8) Bottom-line research answer

If you want the shortest research-based summary:

- **Yes**, people in more interesting/meaningful/autonomous jobs generally report **higher job satisfaction and better well-being**.
- These intrinsic job qualities are usually **stronger predictors** of job satisfaction than pay level.
- **Pay matters**, but its effect is typically **modest** compared with work design and fit, especially at already high income levels.
- **Leadership opportunities** can increase satisfaction if they align with your values and identity, but they also bring stress and are not universally better.
- On **regret**, direct evidence is weaker; satisfaction/fit data are much stronger than regret data.

---

## 9) A short reading list

If you want to look at the research directly, these are good anchors:

1. **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**  
   Meta-analysis of work design and employee outcomes.

2. **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**  
   Meta-analysis of the relationship between pay and job satisfaction.

3. **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**  
   Meta-analysis of person-environment fit.

4. **Allan et al. (2019)**  
   Meta-analysis/review on meaningful work and its outcomes.

5. **Deci, Koestner, & Ryan (1999)**  
   Meta-analysis on extrinsic rewards and intrinsic motivation.

6. **Cerasoli, Nicklin, & Ford (2014)**  
   Meta-analysis comparing intrinsic motivation and extrinsic incentives for performance.

7. **Kosteas (2011)**  
   Promotions and job satisfaction.

8. **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)**, plus **Killingsworth (2021)**  
   Income and well-being.

---

If you want, I can turn this into a **decision framework grounded in the literature**—for example, a weighted scorecard using:
- interest/meaning
- leadership growth
- autonomy
- expected hours/burnout risk
- pay delta
- person-role fit
- option value for future career paths.

answer:
Yes. The closest research is not usually framed as “interesting vs uninteresting jobs,” but as:

- **intrinsic job characteristics**: variety, challenge, autonomy, meaningfulness
- **intrinsic motivation**
- **person–job fit**
- **meaningful work**
- **pay and promotions**
- **regret / career decision-making**

The broad finding is:

> **On average, people are more satisfied in work that is intrinsically interesting, meaningful, and autonomous than in work that merely pays somewhat more.**  
> The caveat is that **fit matters a lot**: if leading a team is a genuine long-term aspiration, that can itself be a major source of meaning and satisfaction.

## 1) Interesting work is a strong predictor of job satisfaction

A large literature in organizational psychology says that jobs feel better when they have:

- **skill variety**
- **task significance**
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**
- sometimes also **task identity** and **social support**

This goes back to the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham), and later meta-analyses found these characteristics are strongly related to:

- **job satisfaction**
- **internal motivation**
- **engagement**
- lower **turnover intentions**

A frequently cited meta-analysis:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**  
  Meta-analysis of work design literature.  
  Main result: “motivational” job characteristics like autonomy, variety, and significance are robustly associated with job satisfaction and motivation.

So if by “interesting” you mean intellectually engaging, varied, challenging, and meaningful, the research is very favorable to it.

## 2) Meaningful work is linked not just to job satisfaction, but to broader well-being

There is also a separate literature on **meaningful work**. Meta-analytic evidence finds that people who experience their work as meaningful tend to report:

- higher **job satisfaction**
- higher **life satisfaction**
- better **general well-being**
- less **burnout**

A representative reference:

- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)**, meta-analysis on meaningful work outcomes.

The important nuance: the link to **job satisfaction** is usually stronger than the link to **overall life happiness**, but both are positive.

So if your Google job is genuinely much more interesting/meaningful, the literature would generally predict **higher day-to-day work satisfaction** there.

## 3) Pay matters, but usually less than people think once income is already high

There is a well-known meta-analysis on this:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**  
  “The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis…”

Their central result is that the relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but fairly small**. The commonly cited correlation is around **r ≈ .15**.

That means:

- higher pay does help
- but **it explains only a small part** of why people like their jobs
- and it is usually **much weaker** than intrinsic job qualities like autonomy, meaningfulness, and fit

This is especially relevant in your case because you’re not comparing “financial insecurity” vs “security.” You’re comparing **two already-elite jobs**, with a **modest pay increase** on one side. Research generally suggests that at that level, a small-to-moderate salary bump often does **less** for satisfaction than differences in the quality of the work itself.

## 4) Autonomy, competence, and relatedness are core predictors of well-being at work

This comes from **Self-Determination Theory** (Deci & Ryan) and its workplace literature.

The main finding is that people do better when work satisfies three basic psychological needs:

- **autonomy**: I have choice/control
- **competence**: I’m good at this and growing
- **relatedness**: I matter to and connect with others

Workplace reviews/meta-analyses consistently find that these are linked to:

- higher well-being
- higher engagement
- lower burnout

This matters because the two jobs may trade off differently:

- the **more interesting Google role** may score high on **competence** and possibly autonomy
- the **OpenAI leadership role** may score higher on **relatedness**, impact, and perhaps a different form of autonomy

So from a research perspective, “less interesting” does not automatically mean “worse” if the new role satisfies important needs you currently lack.

## 5) Person–job fit is one of the biggest variables here

This is very relevant to your specific situation.

A major meta-analysis:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**  
  Meta-analysis of person–job / person–organization fit

Main result: better fit is associated with:

- higher job satisfaction
- higher commitment
- lower intent to quit

And here is the key point:

> You said leading a small team is “something I always wanted to do.”

That means the OpenAI job is not just “less interesting but slightly better paid.” It may represent **better fit with a long-standing aspiration**.

Research does not support a simple rule like “always choose the more interesting technical work.” It supports something more like:

- intrinsic interest matters a lot
- but **fit with core motives and identity** matters a lot too

If “I want to lead” is central to your identity, that can materially affect future satisfaction.

## 6) Promotions/leadership roles are not a guaranteed happiness upgrade

The literature on promotions and managerial roles is more mixed.

Broadly:

- promotions often increase **status**, **pay**, and sometimes short-term satisfaction
- but they can also increase:
  - workload
  - role conflict
  - administrative burden
  - stress
  - time pressure

Longitudinal studies often find that the emotional payoff from status changes is **smaller and less durable** than people expect.

So the research would not say “team lead is better because it’s upward.” It would say:

- leadership is good **if** you value leadership itself and the actual work of management
- it is not good merely because it is a promotion

This is important because many technically strong people discover they wanted **influence**, not necessarily **managerial work**. Those are not the same.

## 7) What about regret?

This is the weakest part of the evidence, because there is not much direct research saying:

- “people who stayed in more interesting IC jobs regretted it more/less than those who took less interesting management roles.”

But there is a robust broader literature on regret:

- **Gilovich & Medvec**: in the short run, people often regret bad actions; in the long run, they more often regret **inactions** and paths not taken, especially ones tied to the **ideal self**

Translated cautiously to your situation:

- If leading is a genuine long-term aspiration, **not trying it** could become a source of regret
- If interesting research work is a core identity value, **leaving it** could also become a source of regret

The literature doesn’t resolve that for you automatically. What it says is:

> Long-run regret is especially likely when people feel they failed to pursue a deeply valued possible self.

So the question is not only “Which job is more pleasant?” but also “Which choice would feel like abandoning a central version of myself?”

## 8) So, what does the evidence suggest overall?

If I compress the literature into a few empirically grounded takeaways:

### Strong findings
1. **Interesting / meaningful / autonomous work strongly predicts job satisfaction.**
2. **Pay matters, but modest differences matter less than intrinsic job quality**, especially at already-high income levels.
3. **Fit with your values and aspirations is crucial.**
4. **Leadership roles are not automatically better for happiness**; they help when they fit your motives.
5. **Regret research suggests people often regret not pursuing important aspirations**, though this is less job-specific evidence.

## 9) Applied specifically to your setup

Without giving a personal opinion, the literature would predict roughly this:

- If the OpenAI offer is only **a bit higher salary** but the work is **substantially less interesting**, then **on average** that points toward **lower day-to-day job satisfaction**.
- However, if leading a small team is a **deep, long-held aspiration**, that is not a trivial factor; it is exactly the sort of thing that **person–job fit** and **regret** research say can matter a lot.
- So the real research-based comparison is probably not:
  - “interesting work” vs “money”
- but:
  - **interesting technical work** vs **leadership aspiration / identity fit**

That is a much closer contest.

## 10) If you want a research-grounded rule of thumb

A decent summary from the literature is:

> **At high income levels, choose the option with the better daily work experience unless the other option fulfills a major identity-level aspiration.**

In your case, “leading a small team” sounds like it may be exactly such an aspiration.

## 11) References worth looking up

If you want to dig into the literature, these are good starting points:

- **Hackman, J. R., & Oldham, G. R.** — Job Characteristics Model
- **Humphrey, S. E., Nahrgang, J. D., & Morgeson, F. P. (2007)** — meta-analysis of work design
- **Judge, T. A., Piccolo, R. F., Podsakoff, N. P., Shaw, J. C., & Rich, B. L. (2010)** — pay and job satisfaction meta-analysis
- **Kristof-Brown, A. L., Zimmerman, R. D., & Johnson, E. C. (2005)** — person–environment fit meta-analysis
- **Deci, E. L., & Ryan, R. M.** — Self-Determination Theory
- **Allan, B. A., Batz-Barbarich, C., Sterling, H. M., & Tay, L. (2019)** — meta-analysis on meaningful work
- **Gilovich, T., & Medvec, V. H.** — long-term regret, omission vs commission

If you want, I can also turn this into a **one-page decision matrix based only on research variables**: intrinsic interest, autonomy, meaning, leadership fit, workload, and salary.

answer:
Yes. There is quite a lot of research on this, though it usually uses terms like **intrinsic motivation**, **meaningful work**, **job characteristics**, and **person-job fit**, rather than “interesting job.”

## Short version

The research picture is roughly:

1. **Interesting, meaningful, autonomous work is a strong predictor of job satisfaction and engagement.**
2. **Pay matters, but its link to job satisfaction is usually much smaller** than the link from work design, autonomy, fit, and meaning—especially once compensation is already high enough to feel fair and secure.
3. **For creative/knowledge work, intrinsic interest matters even more** because it predicts persistence, creativity, and quality of performance.
4. **Regret research is less direct**, but long-term regret tends to center on **not pursuing important personal aspirations** more than on leaving money on the table.  
   The twist in your case is that **leading a team is also an aspiration**, so this is not just “interesting work vs money.”

---

## What the literature says

### 1) Interesting work strongly predicts satisfaction and well-being
A foundational line of research is the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham, 1970s). Jobs tend to be more motivating and satisfying when they provide:

- **Skill variety**
- **Task identity**
- **Task significance**
- **Autonomy**
- **Feedback**

The core idea is that jobs are better when they feel **meaningful**, let you feel **responsible** for outcomes, and let you **learn how you’re doing**.

A major meta-analysis of work design research (Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson, 2007) found that these kinds of job characteristics are **robustly associated** with:

- job satisfaction
- internal motivation
- engagement
- lower withdrawal intentions

So: if by “interesting” you mean intellectually engaging, varied, meaningful, and self-directed, then yes—the literature is very consistent that this matters a lot.

---

### 2) “Meaningful work” has a strong relationship with happiness at work
More recent research on **meaningful work** reaches a similar conclusion. Meta-analytic work by **Bryan Allan and colleagues** finds that meaningful work is strongly associated with:

- higher job satisfaction
- greater work engagement
- better overall well-being
- lower turnover intentions

This is one of the more reliable findings in occupational psychology: people do better, psychologically, when their work feels worthwhile and personally significant.

---

### 3) Person-job fit matters a lot
Another important body of evidence is **person-environment fit**, especially **person-job fit** and **person-organization fit**.

A meta-analysis by **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** found that fit is substantially related to:

- job satisfaction
- organizational commitment
- intention to stay
- performance

This matters for your question because “interestingness” is partly about **fit with your motives and identity**. A role can be prestigious or better paid, but if the daily work is a poor fit for what you find engaging, that predicts lower satisfaction.

---

### 4) Pay matters, but less than many people think
There is a classic tendency to overestimate how much salary differences will affect lasting satisfaction.

A meta-analysis by **Judge et al. (2010)** found that the relationship between **pay level and job satisfaction is positive but modest**. In plain English: money helps, but it is usually **not one of the strongest drivers** of whether people like their jobs.

Also relevant:

- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** found that higher income is associated with better life evaluation, though the relationship with day-to-day emotional well-being was weaker and showed diminishing returns.
- **Killingsworth (2021)** argued the relationship may continue beyond earlier suggested plateaus, but still, the key point remains: **marginal dollars typically matter less than people expect once basic security and fairness are met**.

For someone already in a highly paid role at Google, the research would generally predict that a **small-to-moderate salary increase** has less impact on daily well-being than differences in:

- interest in the work
- autonomy
- meaning
- culture
- workload
- fit with long-term goals

---

### 5) For research/creative work, intrinsic interest is especially important
For creative and knowledge-intensive work, intrinsic motivation tends to matter even more.

- **Self-Determination Theory** (Deci & Ryan; applied to work by Gagné & Deci, 2005) shows that people function best when work supports:
  - **autonomy**
  - **competence**
  - **relatedness**
- Research by **Amabile** and others suggests intrinsic motivation is especially important for **creativity**.
- Meta-analytic evidence (e.g. **Cerasoli, Nicklin, & Ford, 2014**) suggests intrinsic motivation is particularly important for performance quality.

That’s relevant because “interesting” work is not just about being happier—it often supports **better work output**, especially in research or engineering roles.

---

### 6) Regret: the evidence is more indirect
There is **less direct research** on “Do people regret choosing the more interesting job over the higher-status/higher-paid one?”

But there is a broader regret literature:

- **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)**: in the short term, people often regret actions more; in the long term, they regret **inactions** more.
- **Roese & Summerville (2005)**: major regrets often involve domains tied to identity and unrealized potential, including career.

The implication is not “always pick the interesting job.”  
It’s more subtle:

> People tend to regret failing to pursue what they see as a meaningful or identity-defining path.

In your case, there are **two** identity-relevant paths:

- doing more interesting research/engineering work
- finally leading a small team

So regret research does not give an automatic answer. It says the more likely source of future regret is whichever option feels like **turning away from a core aspiration**.

---

### 7) Important caveat: interesting work does not protect you from burnout
One of the most important caveats from burnout research is that highly meaningful or interesting jobs can still be bad for well-being if they come with:

- chronic overwork
- low recovery
- low control
- unfairness
- values conflict

Maslach and Leiter’s work is relevant here: burnout is driven not only by meaning, but also by **workload, control, reward, community, fairness, and values**.

So if one option is “less interesting but leadership” and also “much more exhausting,” that matters a lot.  
Likewise, a very interesting role can still become harmful if the pace is unsustainable.

---

## What seems most supported by the evidence

If I strip the literature down to the part most relevant to your decision:

- **Interesting/meaningful work usually predicts greater job satisfaction than modest pay differences do.**
- **Autonomy and fit matter enormously.**
- **For high-end knowledge workers, once pay is already very good, daily work quality tends to dominate salary increments.**
- **Long-term regret is more about not pursuing what matters to your identity than about optimizing compensation.**
- **But “leading a team” is itself an intrinsic/identity goal**, not just a résumé move.

So the research does **not** cleanly say:  
“Stay in the more interesting job.”

It says something closer to:

> People tend to do best when their work combines strong fit, autonomy, meaning, and progress toward personally important aspirations.  
> If money differences are modest, those factors usually matter more.

---

## If you want the most research-grounded framing of your choice

The literature suggests evaluating the two roles on these dimensions:

1. **Interest/meaning of daily work**
2. **Autonomy/control**
3. **Fit with your identity**
4. **Progress toward a core aspiration**  
   (for you, that may include people leadership)
5. **Expected workload/recovery**
6. **Manager/team quality**
7. **Compensation fairness/security**

That would be more evidence-based than reducing it to “interesting vs salary.”

---

## Good papers to look up

- **Hackman & Oldham** — Job Characteristics Model
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — meta-analysis of work design
- **Gagné & Deci (2005)** — Self-Determination Theory and work motivation
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** — meta-analysis of person-environment fit
- **Judge et al. (2010)** — meta-analysis of pay and job satisfaction
- **Allan et al.** — meta-analyses/reviews on meaningful work
- **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)** — regret
- **Roese & Summerville (2005)** — what people regret most
- **Maslach & Leiter** — burnout and work-life mismatch

If you want, I can turn this into a **more concrete decision memo**: a one-page summary of the research translated into questions you can apply directly to your Google vs OpenAI choice.
